id: README
title: How to use this vault
text: How to use this vault 1. Drop this entire Bloom Energy folder inside your Obsidian vault (or open this folder as its own vault). 2. Open 00 Project Home.md — that's your map of content. 3. Make sure these core community plugins are enabled in Obsidian: Templater , Dataview , Tag Wrangler . 4. Use the templates in 06 Templates/ when creating new notes (Templater plugin). 5. Update 07 Daily/ with a note every research session. Folder structure 01 Sources/ — one note per primary source (10-K, press release, news article). Each has a URL. 02 Atomic Notes/ — one note per fact or claim. Always linked to its source. 03 Drafts/ — in-progress deliverables. Built from atomic notes via wiki links . 04 Final Deliverables/ — submitted versions. 05 Questions/ — open research questions. Triage weekly. 06 Templates/ — note templates for Templater plugin. 07 Daily/ — daily research log. Working with Claude Connect Claude to this vault via the Obsidian MCP connector. Once connected, Claude can read all notes and create new ones in the right folders. Useful prompts: "Read all atomic notes tagged #moat and draft a 1-page competitive summary." "Look at my drafts folder and tell me what facts I have not yet cited." "Read all #bear notes and stress-test my investment thesis." "Summarize what I learned this week from the daily notes."

id: 01 Sources/Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture Brochure 2024
title: Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture — Brochure (2024)
text: Bloom Energy Server with Heat Capture — Brochure (2024) Primary product brochure for the Energy Server with Heat Capture , Bloom's Combined Heat and Power (CHP) configuration. The exhaust adapters bolt onto the back of the Power Modules and harness the >350°C exhaust at two connection points for integration with heat-recovery equipment. Bloom offers an installation guide and full turnkey CHP solutions. Headline efficiency (1 MW installation, lifetime average) | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Electrical efficiency | 54% | | Heat recovery efficiency | >36% | | Total CHP efficiency | >90% | | Exhaust temperature | 356–384 °C (avg ~367 °C) | All values modeled, ±5% vs. field data; assume pipeline natural gas at ISO conditions. Three exhaust-heat applications Chilled water (cooling) — exhaust drives an absorption chiller / Vapor Absorption Machine (VAM) to make chilled water, displacing electric chillers. The data-center use. (1 MW yields 175–200 tons of cooling.) Steam generation — 5–15 barg, dry-saturated or superheated, for industrial/pharma/electronics. Hot water — 13,000–16,500 kg/h @ 80 °C for food processing, space heating, facility hot water. A condensing economizer in series adds a minimum +5% efficiency boost. Without CHP, the facility would have burned extra gas or drawn extra electricity for the same heating/cooling. Notes The >90% figure is combined efficiency (electricity + captured, used heat), realized only where a thermal load sits next to the box. On gas with heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. The block size is 325 kW, scalable to hundreds of MW; low CO₂ footprint, negligible NOx/SOx, no particulates.

id: 01 Sources/Kshana Deep Research Q&A June 2026
title: Kshana Deep Research Q&A (June 2026)
text: Kshana Deep Research Q&A (June 2026) AI-generated deep-research answers to follow-up questions 1–14 on Bloom Energy (revenue quality, contracts, unit economics, stack life, FEOC supply chain, capacity, convertible notes). Source for the atomic notes tagged kshana / unverified . ⚠ Reliability caveats (read before citing) Known hallucination present: the doc calls "Khyber and Rubin" Bloom "direct-conversion architectures." They are NVIDIA GPU platforms, not Bloom products (see Bloom way manufacturing automation 10x volume same headcount context / R&D companion). This claim was excluded from the atomic notes. Internal inconsistency: service backlog quoted as both "$14B" (Q5) and "$9B" (Q10). Verified vault figure is ~$14B ( Service backlog $14B FY2025 ). Optimistic claim vs field data: asserts 2020+ stack life of 10–15 years; our verified field data shows median life ~4.9 yrs, max ~7.7, MTTR 4.7 yrs ( Bloom MTTR 1.9 to 4.7 years ). Captured as a forward/management claim, not fact. Many $/kW, ASP, and margin-delta figures are estimates / analyst reconstructions , not disclosed line items. Treat every derived note as a lead to confirm against 10-K/10-Q/8-K, not as settled fact.

id: 01 Sources/Bloom-Brookfield $25B Expansion
title: Brookfield and Bloom Energy Expand AI Infrastructure Partnership to $25B (June 30, 2026)
text: Brookfield and Bloom Energy Expand AI Infrastructure Partnership to $25B (June 30, 2026) Key takeaways Brookfield increases its financing framework for Bloom-powered AI infrastructure projects from $5B to $25B — a fivefold expansion in under 9 months (original deal Oct 13, 2025). Funding comes from Brookfield's dedicated AI Infrastructure Fund (launched Nov 2025, $100B deployment target across AI factories, power, compute, and capital partnerships). Framing: "a new model for AI factories that integrates power, compute, data center infrastructure, and capital from the outset" — Brookfield pitches "electrons to tokens" end-to-end delivery. Bloom CCO Aman Joshi: original deal was "the first phase of a much larger vision"; expansion "reflects the momentum we are seeing in the market, as evidenced by recently announced large-scale deals." Brookfield: >$1T AUM; >$100B already invested in digital infrastructure and clean power. Stock jumped ~10% on the announcement (TIKR/Motley Fool coverage). Analytical notes (ours) This is a financing framework , not firm product orders — same caveat as the ~$20B backlog: it converts as projects are actually built. But it materially de-risks the funding side of deployment. Sharpens the existing revenue-quality question: the related-party/affiliate channel (Brookfield JVs) that was ~half of Q1 2026 revenue now has 5× more capital behind it. Bull: massive validated demand + capital. Bear: even more of Bloom's growth flows through a partner whose projects Bloom helps finance, with the margin split still undisclosed. Site sections affected: Recent news (new top item), Catalysts (Brookfield line: $5B → $25B), Risks (related-party card), Thesis bull point. URL https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260630023022/en/Brookfield-and-Bloom-Energy-Expand-AI-Infrastructure-Partnership-to-%2425-Billion-Fivefold-Increase-to-Build-and-Finance-Rapid-Power-for-AI-Infrastructure

id: 01 Sources/Hunterbrook Short Report and Bloom 8-K Rebuttal
title: Hunterbrook Short Report ("Bloom's Big Lie") and Bloom's 8-K Rebuttal (July 8–9, 2026)
text: Hunterbrook Short Report ("Bloom's Big Lie") and Bloom's 8-K Rebuttal (July 8–9, 2026) The allegations (Hunterbrook Media, July 8, 2026) Hunterbrook disclosed it may hold positions benefiting from a BE decline. Three claims: 1. China-dependent scandium supply. Using trade data, corporate filings, satellite imagery, and interviews, Hunterbrook traced supply routes it says contradict management's repeated statements that Bloom has no China-dependent supply chain. 2. Scandium scarcity math. Bloom's 5 GW production ambition would need ~220 tons of scandium oxide/year vs projected global supply of ~240 tons — nearly the entire world market. 3. Backlog vs firm orders. Challenged the unaudited ~$20B backlog against audited GAAP remaining performance obligations of roughly $492M (updated from the ~$441M we cite from Q1). Stock fell ~6% on publication. Bloom's response (8-K, Item 7.01, July 9, 2026) Categorically rejects claims about financial results and accounting as "false and misleading"; stands behind audited statements (10-K filed Feb 9, 2026; 10-Q filed Apr 29, 2026). Rejects scandium conclusions: sufficient scandium oxide for current demand and backlog; supply and future scaling not dependent on China ; claims supply-chain visibility supporting 25 GW/year of fuel cell production. Stock recovered ~6% on the rebuttal; law-firm "securities fraud investigations" announced (standard post-short-report ambulance chasing, but worth tracking). Analytical notes (ours) This is the 2019 Hindenburg playbook re-run for the AI era — and it targets exactly the two soft spots our Risks section already flags: backlog vs RPO and supply-chain concentration (we flagged MTAR as sole hot-box supplier and FEOC/scandium ITC exposure before this report). The 25 GW/yr visibility claim is new and aggressive (prior public figure: 2 GW capacity by end-2026, stretchable to 5 GW). Treat as management assertion, unverified. Note the RPO figure moved: ~$441M (Q1 10-Q as we recorded) → ~$492M per Hunterbrook's citation. Verify against the Q1 10-Q / Q2 10-Q when filed. Market took Bloom's side within a day — but the report gives the bear case a fresh, dated, citable source. Site sections affected: Recent news (two items: report + rebuttal), Risks (degradation/dilution card or a new supply-chain card), possibly Competition "watch." URLs 8-K summary: https://www.stocktitan.net/sec-filings/BE/8-k-bloom-energy-corp-reports-material-event-fcbd2d56d6b6.html Seeking Alpha: https://seekingalpha.com/news/4612375-bloom-energy-falls-amid-hunterbook-short-report Benzinga rally coverage: https://www.benzinga.com/trading-ideas/movers/26/07/60356223/bloom-energy-shares-rally-as-management-slams-short-report-as-misleading 24/7 Wall St skeptical take: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/09/the-market-rejects-bloom-energy-short-attack-but-should-you-ignore-it-too/

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Rhode Island analogy 2.5 GW CCGT alternative
title: Rhode Island analogy: a 2.5 GW CCGT data center would use water = all Rhode Island showers + air pollution = all Rhode Island cars
text: Rhode Island analogy: a 2.5 GW CCGT data center would use water = all Rhode Island showers + air pollution = all Rhode Island cars KR Sridhar used a vivid analogy on the Q1 call: a 2.5 GW power block (the scale of Project Jupiter) is 'the capacity of the state of Rhode Island in one single data center.' If powered by combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGT), it would use water equal to all residents of Rhode Island showering daily (~1 million showers/day) and produce air pollution equivalent to all cars in Rhode Island, all concentrated at one site. This is the framing Bloom uses to position why community acceptance favors them. The visual is sticky and powerful for the pitch. Tags #bloom #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/R&D declining as percent of revenue
title: R&D dollars rose ~80% but fell from 10.6% to 7.6% of revenue — operating leverage, not rising intensity
text: R&D dollars rose ~80% but fell from 10.6% to 7.6% of revenue — operating leverage, not rising intensity R&D expense rose ~80% from $103.4M (FY2021) to $186.0M (FY2025) while declining from 10.6% to 7.6% of revenue (Q1 2026: $56.8M, 7.6%) [R]. That is operating leverage on a commercialized platform, not rising research intensity. R&D is reported only in aggregate — there is no project-level breakout, so per-project budgets quoted in third-party summaries are invented. Model R&D as a declining percent of revenue. | Period | R&D ($000s) | % of revenue | |---|---|---| | FY2021 | 103,396 | 10.6% | | FY2022 | 150,606 | 12.6% | | FY2023 | 155,865 | 11.7% | | FY2024 | 148,629 | 10.1% | | FY2025 | 185,993 | 9.2% | | Q1 2026 | 56,849 | 7.6% | Tags #bloom #rnd #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/ITC safe-harbored through 2028 worth 12-15B
title: Bloom has "safe-harbored" ITC eligibility through 2028, tied to a ~$12–15B revenue pipeline
text: Bloom has "safe-harbored" ITC eligibility through 2028, tied to a ~$12–15B revenue pipeline Per Kshana research (verify): Bloom is "safe harboring" the 30% Investment Tax Credit for deployments through 2028 , locking credit eligibility (via construction-start / equipment-procurement rules) against a ~$12–15B gross product-revenue pipeline through 2028. This lets Bloom offer more competitive "all-in" pricing to utility/hyperscale customers while defending a target 34% non-GAAP gross margin. Context (confirmed): OBBB gives fuel cells a flat 30% ITC through 2033, then a step-down; "safe harbor" is a real, standard mechanism to lock the current rate for a project before the schedule changes. The $12–15B figure is Bloom's stated pipeline value, not an audited backlog line. ◑ PARTIAL: the safe-harbor mechanism and the ITC schedule are confirmed; the specific "through 2028" scope and $12–15B figure are company/research framing — confirm against Bloom investor materials. Tags #bloom #itc #policy #backlog

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom competes head to head with CCGT in low cost states
title: Bloom now competing head-to-head with combined cycle gas turbines (CCGT) on multi-gigawatt projects
text: Bloom now competing head-to-head with combined cycle gas turbines (CCGT) on multi-gigawatt projects David Sandler (Baird) asked if Bloom is winning against CCGT. KR Sridhar: 'You can clearly see from the stamp sizes of the projects we operate. We are operating in that class. Very clearly. We are no longer the 10 and 20 megawatt systems. We have hundreds of megawatts going into the gigawatts.' Bloom's projects are now at CCGT scale, not just niche on-site applications. Even in low-cost-electricity states, Bloom wins on total value (time-to-power, modularity, no Band-Aids for load following, native 800V DC). This is a structural moat: CCGT cannot match load following, modularity, or DC native output. Tags #bloom #moat #competitor

text: Natural gas sourced from existing utility pipelines (Marcellus, Permian) Bloom doesn't drill, refine, or transport gas. Customers interconnect to the local gas utility's distribution system. Gas originates from US domestic production (Marcellus, Utica, Permian), flows through interstate pipelines (Kinder Morgan, Williams, Energy Transfer), then through local utilities. AEP-Bloom Cheyenne pipeline expected to move ~400M cubic feet/day to that single site. Tags #bloom #tech #customer

text: 325 kW Energy Server block is the modular shippable unit Bloom manufactures and ships a 325 kW base block that can be grouped to reach roughly 200 kW to hundreds of MW. A 100 MW data center buys roughly 300 of these blocks, not one large machine. This modularity is the reason Bloom can manufacture on a production line and deploy in months rather than engineering a bespoke plant each time. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

text: §45V hydrogen PTC: $0.60-$3.00/kg tiers, BOC deadline 1/1/2028 The §45V hydrogen production tax credit preserved its $0.60-$3.00/kg tiers (based on lifecycle GHG via 45VH2-GREET), and the Treasury three-pillar rule survived. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Order of magnitude reduction in field install time
title: Bloom achieved an order-of-magnitude reduction in field installation time (skid-based deployment)
text: Bloom achieved an order-of-magnitude reduction in field installation time (skid-based deployment) Colin Rusch (Oppenheimer) asked about installation cadence. KR Sridhar: 'We shifted from a [requiring] somebody big dirt for concrete, lay the conduits, get the trades people to come there and do all the work to a solution on a skid. That will just show up and get connected with the least amount of work that can happen... We have closed an order of magnitude reduction in the field time that it takes for us to be able to install our systems.' This is what enables the '90 days to 50 MW' speed claim and is a major operational moat. Most competing technologies (turbines, batteries) still require traditional civil + electrical construction sequencing. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Heat Capture CHP raises total efficiency above 90 percent
title: Heat Capture (CHP) reuses the >350°C exhaust to lift total efficiency from 54% to >90%
text: Heat Capture (CHP) reuses the >350°C exhaust to lift total efficiency from 54% to >90% The Energy Server with Heat Capture — a productized configuration Bloom has been rolling out — bolts exhaust adapters onto the Power Modules to harness exhaust at 356–384°C . Reusing that heat lifts the system from 54% electrical to >36% heat recovery to >90% total CHP efficiency [R] (modeled ±5%). The three uses are hot water, steam, and chilled water — the latter via an absorption chiller (VAM), which is the data-center use : it displaces electric chillers and improves PUE (Bloom claims a 10–15% gain), a second dollar saving on top of fuel. Bloom also lists additional electricity generation among possible waste-heat uses, but for data centers the value is cooling, not added power. Caveats: the >90% figure only lands where a thermal load sits next to the box; the chiller is added capital; the heat figures are modeled to ±5%, not field-validated; >350°C is high-grade for cooling/steam but well short of turbine-grade. Treat it as a win-rate input, not a separate revenue line. Tags #bloom #tech #efficiency #chp #heat-capture

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom pricing not benchmarked vs alternatives
title: Bloom does not benchmark pricing against turbines or grid; sells value not commodity
text: Bloom does not benchmark pricing against turbines or grid; sells value not commodity David Arcaro (Morgan Stanley) asked about pricing power. KR Sridhar: 'We don't compare our pricing with engines and turbines. It's apples and oranges. We are creating a completely different value for our customer.' Vikram Bagri (Citi) pushed on price elasticity. Sridhar: 'We don't think about price. We don't think about cost. We don't think about elasticity. We think about meeting the needs and making sure that we win the AI race.' Whether this is sustainable or eventually catches up with them is open, but for now Bloom is operating in a market where speed-to-power matters more than price-per-kWh. Pricing power is real until it isn't. Tags #bloom #financials #moat

text: Q4 2025 service gross margin: 20% non-GAAP (peak quarter) Q4 2025 service segment hit approximately 20% non-GAAP gross margin, the highest service margin in Bloom's history at the time. The sequential decline (20% → 18%) is worth noting but management framed it as expected volatility within an upward trend. Tags #bloom #financials #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Geographic shift 80 percent US backlog now in low cost states
title: US backlog geographic shift: 80% now in low-cost states (vs 80% in CA/Northeast two years ago)
text: US backlog geographic shift: 80% now in low-cost states (vs 80% in CA/Northeast two years ago) KR Sridhar disclosed: 'Two years ago, over 80% of our US backlog was composed of installations in California and the Northeast, traditionally the high cost of power states. But this year, over 80% of our backlog comes from other states with lower power costs.' This is structurally important: Bloom is no longer dependent on high-electricity-rate jurisdictions where the math is easiest. They're now winning in TX, OK, GA, NC, OH, etc. on time-to-power and reliability. The bear argument that Bloom only works in subsidized blue states is broken. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom way manufacturing automation 10x volume same headcount
title: Bloom's manufacturing automation: scaling 10x volume with the same headcount on the shop floor
text: Bloom's manufacturing automation: scaling 10x volume with the same headcount on the shop floor KR Sridhar on the Q1 call described Bloom's manufacturing leverage: 'If you had come to our factory and seen the few hundred people that we have manufacturing our stacks when we were doing 200 megawatts a year. And if you came at the end of this year when we will be doing almost 10x that amount, the number of employees on the shop floor will be the same.' This is the math behind the operating leverage. Same labor cost producing 10x output through automation. The same employees are 'upskilled from manual labor to automation' rather than being replaced, which is a culture point Bloom emphasizes. Tags #bloom #tech #moat #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Inference data centers same Bloom architecture fewer LEGO blocks
title: Bloom's modular architecture means inference data centers use the same product, just fewer LEGO blocks
text: Bloom's modular architecture means inference data centers use the same product, just fewer LEGO blocks Nick Amicucci (Evercore) asked how Bloom plays in inference vs training. KR Sridhar: 'Our exact same architecture with fewer LEGO blocks. With fewer LEGO blocks, with an inference data center multiplied many times over, can power the training data center. No difference. No other technology can do that. That is how we built it.' Inference data centers are smaller individually (powering edge sites) but more numerous (closer to populations). Bloom's modular Server blocks work at any scale. Other technologies (turbines, batteries) don't scale down efficiently. This is a structural advantage for the post-training-buildout era. Tags #bloom #tech #moat #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q2 2026 guidance at least as good as Q1
title: Q2 2026 guidance hint: revenue should be 'at least as good as Q1' (>= $751M)
text: Q2 2026 guidance hint: revenue should be 'at least as good as Q1' (>= $751M) Simon Edwards on the Q1 2026 call: 'After a strong start to the year, and anticipating that Q2 revenue should be at least as good as Q1, we are raising our fiscal 2026 guidance to new levels.' This sets an implicit Q2 floor of ~$751M. With FY guidance midpoint of $3.6B and Q1 actual at $751M, H1 = $1.50B+ and H2 = $2.1B+, implying continued sequential acceleration. Q3/Q4 each at ~$1.05B run rate. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/LEGO block architecture more blocks more reliability
title: Bloom's LEGO block architecture: more blocks = more reliability (large projects MORE reliable than small)
text: Bloom's LEGO block architecture: more blocks = more reliability (large projects MORE reliable than small) Ben Kallo (Baird) asked how customers got comfortable with larger Bloom projects. KR Sridhar: 'The boxes do not know whether they are sitting along with five other boxes or 5,000 other boxes. Nothing in our scaling has scaling risk. In fact, because these are hard swappable LEGO blocks, the more LEGO blocks you have, the more reliable our system gets. So large block power becomes a lot more reliable than small block power.' This is a meaningful counterintuitive point. Adding redundant modular units increases system availability through fault tolerance (N+1 redundancy compounds). A 100 MW Bloom install is statistically MORE reliable than a 10 MW install, contrary to typical industrial machinery scaling. This directly answers the bear concern that 'we haven't seen Bloom at gigawatt scale.' Scaling is just adding LEGO blocks; no engineering risk. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Book and ship double digit percent of revenue
title: Book-and-ship business: 'significant double-digit percentage' of FY2025 revenue
text: Book-and-ship business: 'significant double-digit percentage' of FY2025 revenue Mark Strouse (JPM) asked about book-and-ship (book the order, ship it, turn it on, all within a short window). KR Sridhar: 'In 2025, we had a significant double-digit percentage of book and ship that we were able to do.' Best example: powering a data center in 55 days. Book-and-ship matters for two reasons. (1) It's a competitive advantage when other vendors miss their promises and customers come to Bloom for rescue. (2) It implies Bloom can capture sudden demand that doesn't appear in any backlog. KR expects this to continue at double-digit percentages. Tags #bloom #customer #financials #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/M&A philosophy selective platform extensions only
title: KR on M&A: selective, only for acquisitions that 'make it easier to bring the smart platform to customers'
text: KR on M&A: selective, only for acquisitions that 'make it easier to bring the smart platform to customers' Colin Rusch (Oppenheimer) asked about M&A given the augmented balance sheet and strong stock. KR Sridhar: 'We can be selective about things that matter to us and things that matter to our customers. And if we had some acquisitions, will that make it easier for us to bring that entire smart platform to our customers in a better way... lighting up the planet is a good day job. I don't need another day job.' Framing: organic TAM is so large that M&A is not a priority, but they would do tuck-ins that extend the platform. Aaron Hoover's hire in August 2025 suggests they're at least scanning targets. Worth tracking what tuck-in M&A looks like over the next 12 months. Tags #bloom #m&a #leadership

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 electricity gross margin fell to 24 percent
title: Q1 2026 electricity gross margin fell to 23.9% (from 57.1% Q1 25)
text: Q1 2026 electricity gross margin fell to 23.9% (from 57.1% Q1 25) Electricity segment (own-the-box, sell-the-power) GAAP gross margin compressed to 23.9% in Q1 2026 from 57.1% in Q1 2025. Revenue in the segment fell from $27.0M to $9.9M. The simultaneous revenue contraction AND margin compression reflects Bloom's deliberate exit from owning generation assets in favor of selling boxes. The remaining electricity portfolio carries less favorable economics. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 install gross margin negative 35 percent

text: Q1 2026 installation gross margin: -35.3% (still a loss leader) Installation revenue of $25.9M in Q1 2026 came at a $9.1M GAAP gross loss, a -35.3% margin (-29.4% non-GAAP). Install is structurally low- or negative-margin because Bloom often subsidizes installation to win the product deal. Worth understanding: is install a permanent loss leader, or does it scale to break-even? Compare Q4 2025 install margin of -10.7%. Tags #bloom #financials #bear #needs-verification

id: 02 Atomic Notes/800V DC product launch every server forward ships ready

text: Every Bloom server shipped forward will be 800V DC ready with removable AC adapter (FY2026) KR Sridhar on the Q4 2025 call announced a product transition: 'Starting now, every Bloom server we ship will be 800 volts DC ready. Backward-compatible, future-proofed. This makes Bloom the only on-site power solution that doesn't require external rectifiers, transformers, or power conditioning to interface with Nvidia Rubin and beyond. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

text: Bloom valuation: ~80x book value, EV/EBITDA above 600x, bear PT -43% At a ~$79B market cap, BE trades at roughly 80x book value and an EV/EBITDA above 600x. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Service backlog $14B FY2025
title: Service backlog at YE 2025: approximately $14 billion (separate from $6B product backlog)
text: Service backlog at YE 2025: approximately $14 billion (separate from $6B product backlog) Maciej Kurzymski disclosed on the Q4 2025 call that Bloom had approximately $14 billion in service backlog at year-end 2025, separate from the $6 billion product backlog. Total backlog ~$20 billion. This is the cleanest single number explaining Bloom's annuity revenue stream. With 100% attach rate and 10-15 year service contracts on data centers, every new product booking adds proportionally to this service backlog. Service backlog is what justifies a multiple expansion if you believe service can keep its 20% non-GAAP gross margin and scale. Tags #bloom #financials #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/30 percent ITC LCOE impact 3 cents per kWh
title: 30% ITC ≈ $2,250/kW capex relief ≈ 3¢/kWh LCOE reduction
text: 30% ITC ≈ $2,250/kW capex relief ≈ 3¢/kWh LCOE reduction Unsubsidized Bloom system capex runs ~$6,640-$8,000/kW. The 30% §48E ITC delivers roughly $2,250/kW of capex relief on a ~$7,500/kW system, which works out to ~3¢/kWh levelized reduction in delivered electricity cost. This is what closes the gap between an unsubsidized $0.13-0.16/kWh Bloom megawatt and the ~$0.10/kWh average US commercial grid price. Tags #regulation #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Service annuity 127M per GW-year
title: Service revenue runs at roughly $127M per deployed GW-year — the annuity that compounds with the fleet
text: Service revenue runs at roughly $127M per deployed GW-year — the annuity that compounds with the fleet The service segment is a long-dated annuity driven by cumulative deployed capacity , not current-year sales. Calibrated from FY2025 service revenue over cumulative GW, the factor is ≈ $127M per GW-year [D]. Every gigawatt shipped adds a maintenance and stack-replacement stream that pays for years, at ~13% GAAP margin (recently inflected). With a 100% attach rate and ~$14B service backlog, this is the lowest-variance line in the model — it survives a slow quarter and compounds as the installed base grows. In the driver tree: Service = Cum_GW × $127M/GW·yr . Tags #bloom #service #moat #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 revenue mix product 87 percent

text: Q1 2026 revenue mix: Product 87%, Service 8%, Install 3%, Electricity 1% Q1 2026 revenue breakdown by segment: Product $653.3M (87.0%), Service $61.9M (8.2%), Installation $25.9M (3.5%), Electricity $9.9M (1.3%). id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 capacity revised to 5 GW current footprint
title: MAJOR REVISION: Bloom's current manufacturing footprint will allow 5 GW annual delivery (not 2 GW)
text: MAJOR REVISION: Bloom's current manufacturing footprint will allow 5 GW annual delivery (not 2 GW) On the Q1 2026 call, KR Sridhar revised the capacity narrative materially: 'Our current manufacturing footprint will allow us to deliver 5 gigawatts of product annually. We will expand to that capacity and meet the delivery dates needed by our customers.' This supersedes the earlier '2 GW by YE 2026' framing from the FY2025 results. Bloom is no longer claiming a fixed capacity ceiling; they're claiming the current physical footprint can deliver up to 5 GW/year. Beyond that, the 'copy exact' manufacturing model allows faster expansion than any peer. Material because: (1) it removes the 'capacity bottleneck' bear argument, (2) it directly supports the raised 2026 revenue guidance ($3.4-3.8B), and (3) it implies Bloom can take more share of the AI buildout than previously assumed. text: Bloom SOFC exhaust ~10x CO2 concentration of a gas turbine Solid oxide fuel cell exhaust is roughly 10 times more concentrated in CO2 than gas turbine exhaust. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Absorption chillers 20 percent electricity reduction
title: Bloom absorption chiller solution can reduce data center electricity usage by 20%+
text: Bloom absorption chiller solution can reduce data center electricity usage by 20%+ Manav Gupta (UBS) asked KR Sridhar about combined heat and power on the Q4 2025 call. Sridhar's answer: 'We think we can reduce electricity usage in the data center by at least 20%. Two zero. That's a big number for these huge power-hungry gigawatt-class data centers.' The absorption chiller takes Bloom's 800°C+ waste heat and produces chilled water at ~5°C / 40°F for liquid cooling loops. It also eliminates HFC refrigerants used in vapor compression (a major global warming contributor) and avoids the chiller supply chain bottleneck. Tags #bloom #tech #moat #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/2 GW by Dec 2026 supports 4x FY2025 revenue Q3 context

text: Q3 2025 capacity framing: 2 GW by Dec 2026 supports ~4x FY2025 revenue (later revised UP to 5 GW) On the Q3 2025 call, KR Sridhar said: 'We are doubling our capacity to two gigawatts by December 2026, which will support about four times our 2025 revenue.' With FY2025 at $2.02B, 4x = $8.08B. This is the steady-state revenue capability of the 2 GW footprint. By Q1 2026, this was REVISED UP: Sridhar said current footprint actually supports 5 GW. Watch the progression: Q3 2025 (2 GW / $8B) → Q1 2026 (5 GW / ~$20B implied). The 2.5x capacity revision is material. Tags #bloom #financials #moat

text: Bloom has 100% attach rate between product sales and service contracts KR Sridhar: 'We have a 100% attach rate between our product sales and our service. That's the first place to start. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Ultracapacitors replace battery banks for load following
title: Bloom uses ultracapacitors for load following, eliminating battery banks
text: Bloom uses ultracapacitors for load following, eliminating battery banks Bloom's solution combines fuel cells with ultracapacitors for load following. The advantages over battery banks: no decay, no replacement cycle, no heat generation, lower cost. Manav Gupta (UBS) raised this on the Q1 2026 call as a competitive differentiator: 'Your solution with ultracapacitor and high reliability needs minimal battery backup, in some cases, no battery backup.' KR Sridhar confirmed and went further: 'In our combined solution between our fuel cells and our ultra caps' the full microgrid solution doesn't need batteries. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

text: Q3 2025 service gross margin: 14.4% (2nd consecutive double-digit, 7th consecutive profitable) Service margin was 14.4% in Q3 2025, the second straight quarter of double-digit margin and the seventh straight quarter of service profitability. Subsequent quarters: Q4 2025 at 20%, Q1 2026 at 18% (non-GAAP). The trajectory: ~1% (Q1 25) → ~5% → 14% (Q3) → 20% (Q4) → 18% (Q1 26). Steady inflection with one quarter of choppiness. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Utility channel rate basing now possible
title: Utility channel opening: regulators now allow utilities to rate-base Bloom solutions
text: Utility channel opening: regulators now allow utilities to rate-base Bloom solutions Ben Kallo (Baird) asked about new sales channels. KR Sridhar: 'Now with the utility scale customers, for the first time, I think they are seeing favorable regulation that allows them to rate base and offer better solutions to their customers. So we see a strong interest coming from both gas utilities and electric utilities to say in the face of that favorable regulatory and price design environment, can we partner with you.' This is a new and potentially large channel. Utilities buying Bloom systems to rate-base means the utility (not the end customer) pays Bloom, and the utility recovers its investment through customer rates. Removes the customer financing question entirely. Tags #bloom #customer #regulation

id: 02 Atomic Notes/800V DC inevitability copper transformer constraints
title: 800V DC architecture is inevitable; world doesn't have enough copper and transformers for AC at AI scale
text: 800V DC architecture is inevitable; world doesn't have enough copper and transformers for AC at AI scale Manav Gupta (UBS) and KR Sridhar discussed the 800V DC transition on the Q1 2026 call. Sridhar: 'It's self-evident to me that, that is going to come. It's inevitable that they're going to switch to that. Because the world does not have enough copper... the world does not have enough transformers. So necessity is going to force them there. And once they try it, they will not go back on it.' Nvidia's next-generation rack designs (Rubin and beyond) require 800V DC. Bloom's product readiness for direct 800V DC output skips the AC-to-DC conversion losses inside hyperscale data centers and bypasses the transformer/copper supply chain bottleneck. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

Tags #bloom #customer #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Backlog 20B vs product RPO 441M reconciliation
title: Only ~$441M of the ~$20B "backlog" is GAAP RPO — the rest is framework pipeline, not firm obligation
text: Only ~$441M of the ~$20B "backlog" is GAAP RPO — the rest is framework pipeline, not firm obligation Per Kshana research (verify vs 10-Q): the ~ $20B backlog and the ~$441M product Remaining Performance Obligation (RPO) measure very different things. RPO (ASC 606) counts only firm, non-cancellable product commitments. The gap is driven by: Service (~$14B): excluded via the "right-to-invoice" practical expedient (recurring monthly maintenance), despite the 100% attach rate. Product (~$6B): includes anticipated ITC and incentives , which aren't customer "unearned revenue" until a system is commissioned. Framework deals (Oracle/AEP/AWS): secure manufacturing slots and "pipeline velocity" but only convert to RPO as specific POs are executed. Takeaway: the $20B is opportunity/pipeline, not contracted revenue — backlog-to-RPO conversion is "lumpy." This directly answers the backlog-quality question (framework ceiling ≠ firm order book). ⚠ Research-derived; confirm the exact RPO figure and composition against the 10-Q. Tags #bloom #backlog #revenue-quality

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 service gross margin inflection 13 percent
title: Q1 2026 service gross margin: 13.3% (vs 1.3% Q1 25) - service finally inflecting
text: Q1 2026 service gross margin: 13.3% (vs 1.3% Q1 25) - service finally inflecting Service segment GAAP gross margin jumped to 13.3% in Q1 2026 from just 1.3% in Q1 2025, a 12-percentage-point swing. Non-GAAP service gross margin was 18.0% (vs 4.8% prior year). This is one of the most important Q1 numbers. Service has been the historically weakest segment because of warranty and stack-replacement costs. The inflection validates Bloom's argument that the installed base produces durable recurring margin as it scales. Tags #bloom #financials #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/FuelCell Energy MCFC sub-scale
title: FuelCell Energy is MCFC (not SOFC), sub-scale, perpetual losses
text: FuelCell Energy is MCFC (not SOFC), sub-scale, perpetual losses FuelCell Energy uses molten carbonate fuel cells (MCFC), not SOFC. Their Torrington facility produces only 41 MW annualized vs 100 MW nameplate. The company has carried 24 consecutive years of operating losses. Not a credible primary-power competitor for AI data centers at gigawatt scale. Tags #competitor #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom 17-year SOFC head start
title: 17-year SOFC head start vs nearest competitor
text: 17-year SOFC head start vs nearest competitor Bloom's first commercial install was Google Mountain View in July 2008. The nearest Western SOFC peer (Doosan Fuel Cell, Ceres-licensed) began mass production in July 2025. That's ~17 years of head start, ~1.8 GW of installed fleet, and a manufacturing line that's 20× any competitor. This is the core 'why hasn't anyone caught up' answer. Tags #bloom #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Brookfield JV product margin 400-600 bps below third-party
title: Brookfield-JV product margin runs an estimated 400–600 bps below peak third-party sales
text: Brookfield-JV product margin runs an estimated 400–600 bps below peak third-party sales Per Kshana research ( estimate , not disclosed): sales through the Brookfield "AI Fund" framework carry a product gross margin roughly 400–600 basis points below peak arm's-length third-party sales — the delta reflecting the financing-framework/shared-project economics versus the "time-to-power" premium third parties pay. This is the first quantified answer to the previously-undisclosed related-vs-third-party margin split (our #1 open risk), but it is an analyst reconstruction, not a filing line item. Kshana argues the lower gross margin is offset by operating leverage (manufacturing overhead absorption) — Q1 2026 operating margin reportedly reached 17.3% (+~1,300 bps). Bloom's stated 2026 target is a 34% non-GAAP gross margin . ⚠ Estimate only; Bloom does NOT disclose the margin split. Treat as a working assumption to pressure-test, not fact. Tags #bloom #related-party #margin #revenue-quality

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom NG-SOFC emissions at gas turbine parity
title: Bloom NG-SOFC lifecycle emissions: 679-839 lb CO2/MWh (parity with gas turbine)
text: Bloom NG-SOFC lifecycle emissions: 679-839 lb CO2/MWh (parity with gas turbine) Bloom's natural-gas-fed Energy Server has lifecycle emissions roughly 679-839 lb CO2/MWh, which is at parity with a modern combined-cycle gas plant, not zero. Roughly 91% of installed units run on natural gas (not biogas/hydrogen). The carbon story only fully works with biogas or hydrogen feedstock OR with the §45Q-monetized carbon capture skid. Don't overclaim emissions advantage in NG mode. Tags #bloom #tech #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/AEP contract cost-recovery clause
title: The AEP deal carries a cost-recovery clause that compensates AEP if conditions aren't met
text: The AEP deal carries a cost-recovery clause that compensates AEP if conditions aren't met The AEP agreement grew from a 100 MW pilot to a firm 1 GW / $2.65B, ~15–20-year PPA . Its costs are recovered through special contracts with AEP's large data-center customers (not traditional utility rate base), which de-risks adoption for the utility — the counterparties, not AEP, bear the cost. The long PPA gives Bloom long-dated product + service revenue visibility. Kshana additionally claims a specific cost-recovery clause entitling AEP to compensation for incurred costs if conditions aren't met by Q2 2026. That specific clause is not corroborated in public sources — treat as unconfirmed. ◑ PARTIAL: the "costs recovered via special large-customer contracts" structure and ~15–20-yr PPA are confirmed ( Utility Dive · Power Engineering ); the specific Q2-2026 compensation clause is unverified. Tags #bloom #contracts #risk

id: 02 Atomic Notes/CFO transition Maciej to Simon Edwards Feb to Apr 2026

text: CFO transition: Maciej Kurzymski (acting) at Feb 2026 → Simon Edwards confirmed CFO by April 2026 At the Q4 2025 call on Feb 5, 2026, Maciej Kurzymski was introduced as 'acting principal financial officer and also our principal accounting officer.' Tags #bloom #leadership #needs-verification

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Brookfield equity method loss 40M 2025
title: Brookfield JV produced a $40.4M equity-method loss in 2025
text: Brookfield JV produced a $40.4M equity-method loss in 2025 The same Brookfield Asset Management relationship that anchors the AI-financing story also generated a $40.4M equity-method loss for Bloom in 2025. Q1 2026 added another $17.0M of equity in loss of unconsolidated affiliates from the Brookfield JVs. This is a real friction in the bull case: the partnership doesn't yet generate Bloom equity income. It generates Bloom equity losses (currently), even as it monetizes the AI capex thesis. Tags #bloom #financials #bear #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/MTAR Technologies single-source Hot Box supplier
title: MTAR Technologies (India) is a single-source "Hot Box" supplier — a supply-chain single point of failure
text: MTAR Technologies (India) is a single-source "Hot Box" supplier — a supply-chain single point of failure Per Kshana research (verify): the Hot Box — the core high-temperature assembly of the Bloom Energy Server — is built by MTAR Technologies in India . India is not a covered nation (so it's FEOC-clean), but the concentration is a single-source dependency : the "copy exact" architecture requires MTAR to match Bloom's cadence precisely, so any geopolitical or operational disruption at MTAR would bottleneck Bloom's entire ramp. This is a distinct risk from FEOC exposure: operational/concentration risk on a critical component. MTAR has supplied Bloom's hot boxes (and electrolyzer units) for 9+ years as sole supplier , and is scaling capacity from 8,000 units/yr → 12,000 (FY26) → 20,000 (FY27) → 30,000 (FY28) , with Bloom co-investing in that expansion — so the dependency is deep and deliberate, and MTAR's ability to scale in lockstep gates Bloom's delivery timelines. ✓ CONFIRMED via India trade/analyst coverage (sole hot-box supplier, capacity ramp, Bloom investment). Sources: T&D India · Hydrogen Fuel News Tags #bloom #supply-chain #risk #manufacturing

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom not order or capacity constrained Q1 2026
title: Bloom is currently neither order constrained nor capacity constrained
text: Bloom is currently neither order constrained nor capacity constrained Direct quote from KR Sridhar Q1 2026 call: 'Today, we are not order constrained and not capacity constrained. The pace of our revenue growth is decided by how fast our customers can build their greenfield sites, not how fast we can power them.' This is a striking framing. The revenue ceiling is now SET BY CUSTOMER SITE READINESS, not by Bloom's ability to deliver. This is the inverse of the traditional power industry bottleneck. It also implies that any acceleration in customer site construction (better permitting, faster construction practices) flows directly to Bloom revenue. Tags #bloom #moat #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Customer concentration top 3 equals 53 percent
title: Top 3 customers = 53% of FY2024 revenue; #1 is a related party
text: Top 3 customers = 53% of FY2024 revenue; #1 is a related party Per the FY2024 10-K, the top three customers represented 53% of FY2024 revenue (~23% / 16% / 14%). The #1 customer is SK ecoplant, a related party (not an arm's-length buyer). Real customer concentration risk. Especially fragile if any one renegotiates pricing. Tags #bloom #customer #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom $4B accumulated deficit
title: Bloom has carried a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit; never sustainably profitable
text: Bloom has carried a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit; never sustainably profitable After ~17 years of operation, Bloom has a ~$4.0 billion accumulated deficit and has never sustainably earned its cost of capital. Q1 2026 net income of $70.65M was the first sustained profitable quarter. Question the durability of this profitability if AI capex slows or pricing renegotiates downward. Tags #bloom #financials #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/More than half data center backlog non-Oracle
title: More than half of data center backlog comes from non-Oracle hyperscalers, neoclouds, and colocation providers
text: More than half of data center backlog comes from non-Oracle hyperscalers, neoclouds, and colocation providers KR Sridhar: 'Well more than half of our current data center backlog comes from other hyperscalers, neo clouds and colocation providers.' These are described as following the same '100% Bloom' microgrid model as Oracle Project Jupiter: 'No grid, no dirty diesel generators for backup, no battery banks for load following, no engines, no turbines, just Bloom and Bloom alone.' This materially reduces the 'Oracle concentration' bear argument. The $20B backlog is more diversified than the headline deal list suggests. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/PEM efficiency 40-60 percent backup only
title: PEM (Plug Power) efficiency 40-60%; backup/mobility, not baseload
text: PEM (Plug Power) efficiency 40-60%; backup/mobility, not baseload Plug Power's PEM fuel cells operate at 40-60% efficiency depending on application. PEM has <1 min start-up (vs SOFC ~60 min) but requires pure hydrogen. Suited for mobility and backup, not primary baseload power for data centers. Different market entirely. Tags #competitor #tech

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Trillion cell hours 6 billion data points per day
title: Bloom has over 1 trillion cell hours of field operation data; 6 billion data points per day
text: Bloom has over 1 trillion cell hours of field operation data; 6 billion data points per day KR Sridhar on the Q4 2025 call: 'We have a few trillion cell hours of field operation... More than 6 billion data points come from our field to us every single day. We are using AI... to improve our performance every single day. Because we have a digital twin associated to every single fuel cell stack and data from the real field is coming and feeding the digital twin.' This is the data moat. A new SOFC competitor would need a decade of installed base operating in the field to accumulate comparable data. The digital twin per stack is what enables predictive maintenance and is a major reason service margin has scaled. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/45Q carbon capture credit values
title: §45Q preserved and enhanced: $85/t industrial CCS, $180/t DAC
text: §45Q preserved and enhanced: $85/t industrial CCS, $180/t DAC OBBBA spared and enhanced the §45Q carbon capture credit: $85/tonne for industrial CCS (Bloom-Chart pathway), $180/tonne for direct air capture saline. EOR/utilization rates leveled up to $85/$180. Unlocks SOFC+capture economics directly. Tags #regulation #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Inference TAM larger than training but at edge
title: Inference will require more total power than training, but distributed at the edge near population centers
text: Inference will require more total power than training, but distributed at the edge near population centers Nick Amicucci (Evercore) asked about training vs inference. KR Sridhar: 'Influence [inference] is going to be much bigger than training in terms of total gigawatt need. But it is going to be not concentrated in the multi-gigawatt data centers... Inference, by definition, is at the edge, a lot closer to highly dense populations of people and processes.' This is the structural reason Bloom thinks the AI power TAM keeps growing AFTER training capex peaks: inference workloads will need clean, quiet, water-efficient power in cities and suburbs where turbines aren't feasible. This is the bull case for Bloom's TAM extending past the first AI buildout wave. Tags #bloom #moat #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Customer prepayments to reserve capacity
title: Customers are paying prepayments to reserve future Bloom capacity
text: Customers are paying prepayments to reserve future Bloom capacity Simon Edwards on Q1 2026 cash flow: 'Even with those investments [working capital build], cash flow from operating activities was an inflow of $73.6 million... driven by a step change in profitability, strong collections and customer prepayments to reserve capacity.' Customers are now paying upfront to lock in capacity slots. This is a meaningful demand signal: customers paying cash today for delivery later is the strongest possible commitment. It also helps Bloom's working capital cycle. Tags #bloom #customer #financials #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bridge power conversation no longer exists
title: Bloom: 'bridge power' conversation no longer exists with hyperscalers
text: Bloom: 'bridge power' conversation no longer exists with hyperscalers Nick Amicucci asked whether hyperscalers want to use Bloom as bridge power then convert to grid later. KR Sridhar: 'Earlier in this conversation, they used to bring up the concept of Bridge power with us. And I would smile and always say we're happy to sell you a bridge to a bridge because [the grid is not] coming, okay? So today, that conversation is nonexisting.' Meaningful signal: customers have stopped framing Bloom as a temporary solution. The 10-15 year service contracts also support this. This addresses the 'temporary bridge vs permanent layer' open question in part. Tags #bloom #moat #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/800V DC water through straw analogy CPU to Rubin
title: 800V DC 'water through a straw' analogy: from 13 kW CPU racks to 650-1300 kW Rubin AI racks
text: 800V DC 'water through a straw' analogy: from 13 kW CPU racks to 650-1300 kW Rubin AI racks KR Sridhar's most elegant 800V DC explanation (Q3 2025 call): 'Power coming into the rack is like a hungry human being drinking water through a straw. The 48V DC was sufficient when CPU racks were 13 kW. They put a lot of band-aids on it to make sure Blackwell chips that come somewhere near the 130 kW can handle it. With a straw. Guess what? The Rubin chips and going forward are going to be five times that to 10 times that.' Rack power trajectory: Legacy CPU: 13 kW Nvidia Blackwell: ~130 kW (10x) Nvidia Rubin and beyond: 650-1300 kW (5-10x Blackwell) To deliver that much power through the same physical rack space, voltage MUST go up (laws of physics). 800V DC is the only viable answer. Bloom is the only on-site solution that produces 800V DC natively because it was architected for DC from day one in 2000. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/2000+ employees US manufactured
title: Bloom employs 2,000+ worldwide; systems manufactured in the United States
text: Bloom employs 2,000+ worldwide; systems manufactured in the United States Per the Q1 2026 release: Bloom employs more than 2,000 people worldwide and manufactures its systems in the United States. Headquartered in San Jose / Silicon Valley. The US-manufacturing footprint matters for the FEOC and tariff risks: Bloom is naturally domestic-content-compliant in a way most clean energy hardware competitors are not. Tags #bloom #tech #moat #regulation

id: 02 Atomic Notes/2026 guidance revenue $3.4-3.8B
title: FY2026 guidance: Revenue $3.4-3.8B (+80% YoY midpoint), non-GAAP gross margin ~34%
text: FY2026 guidance: Revenue $3.4-3.8B (+80% YoY midpoint), non-GAAP gross margin ~34% Bloom raised its FY2026 guidance: revenue $3.4 to $3.8 billion (~80% YoY at the midpoint, up from prior guidance of ~60%), non-GAAP gross margin ~34% (+4pp YoY), non-GAAP operating income $600M to $750M (~3.1x YoY), non-GAAP EPS $1.85 to $2.25 (~2.7x YoY), adjusted EBITDA $650M to $800M. The midpoint of revenue guidance is $3.6B, implying a ~$2.85B run rate exit on roughly $750M Q1 base. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q4 2025 EBITDA flat YoY at $146M
title: Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA $146.1M (flat YoY vs Q4 2024's $147.3M)
text: Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA $146.1M (flat YoY vs Q4 2024's $147.3M) Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $146.1M, essentially flat versus Q4 2024's $147.3M. Q4 2025 operating income $133M vs Q4 2024's $133.4M. Non-GAAP EPS $0.45 vs $0.43 (modest growth). This is an underappreciated nuance: while FY2025 EBITDA grew significantly, Q4 2025 EBITDA was flat YoY. The growth was front-loaded into Q1-Q3 2025. Q1 2026 then snapped EBITDA back to $143M but vs a Q1 2025 base of only $25M, so the YoY math looks dramatic. Tags #bloom #financials #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Hyperscale customer count 6 vs 1 year earlier
title: Hyperscale + neo-cloud customer count: 'half a dozen' at YE 2025, up from one a year earlier
text: Hyperscale + neo-cloud customer count: 'half a dozen' at YE 2025, up from one a year earlier KR Sridhar on the Q4 2025 call: 'The backlog we reported today includes half a dozen hyperscale and neo cloud end customers, compared to just one a year ago.' This 6x growth in named hyperscale customers in twelve months directly counters the 'overdependent on Oracle' bear narrative. Customer count diversification is what reduces concentration risk over time, and it's growing fast. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Global power shortage Frankfurt Munich Dublin Taipei Tokyo Delhi Mumbai
title: Global AI power shortage observed in Frankfurt, Munich, Dublin, Taipei, Tokyo, Delhi, Mumbai
text: Global AI power shortage observed in Frankfurt, Munich, Dublin, Taipei, Tokyo, Delhi, Mumbai KR Sridhar on international demand (Q3 2025): 'Whether it is Frankfurt, whether it is Munich, whether it is Dublin, whether it is Taipei. They all have a power shortage problem. And they all clearly recognize that their central power plants along with transmission distribution cannot keep up with AI speed. This is true in Delhi. This is true in Mumbai.' Key shift in Tokyo: natural gas viewed as long-term solution (not just bridge) after US LNG agreements with allies. 'Once that policy unlock happens, we think there will be a tremendous acceleration in those places.' KR was personally visiting these capitals; suggests strong commercial engagement preceding visible deals. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q4 2025 year-end cash $2.5B inventory $643M
title: Q4 2025 year-end balance: $2.5B total cash, $643M inventory, $113.9M Q4 cash from ops
text: Q4 2025 year-end balance: $2.5B total cash, $643M inventory, $113.9M Q4 cash from ops Bloom ended FY2025 with $2.5B total cash on the balance sheet (boosted significantly by the October 2025 $2.5B zero-coupon convertible). Year-end inventory was $643M, 'slightly higher than expected as we prepare for a strong 2026.' Q4 2025 cash from operating activities was $113.9M; capex $57M. Bloom is sitting on a war chest while building inventory for the demand surge. Q1 2026 then drew inventory down to $732M (built further), and cash to $2.49B. The cap stack is in place for the FY2026 scale-up. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Continuous capacity additions hundreds of MW per quarter
title: Capacity expansion shifted to continuous additions of 'hundreds of megawatts per quarter'
text: Capacity expansion shifted to continuous additions of 'hundreds of megawatts per quarter' Bloom changed its capacity expansion philosophy from lumpy one-off additions (e.g. doubling capacity over 12 months) to continuous additions of 'hundreds of megawatts a quarter.' KR Sridhar: 'Based on demand profile, we have now shifted to adding capacity continuously. Hundreds of megawatts a quarter as opposed to lumpy one-off additions to be completed in a year's time.' This is operationally significant: it implies a manufacturing process flexible enough to add capacity in months, not years. It also implies real-time matching of capacity to demand rather than over- or under-building. Tags #bloom #moat #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/7 AI ecosystem channels framework
title: Bloom's AI strategy is built on 7 distinct ecosystem channels, each with a lighthouse customer
text: Bloom's AI strategy is built on 7 distinct ecosystem channels, each with a lighthouse customer On the Q3 2025 call, KR Sridhar laid out an explicit framework: 'We are embedded in seven distinct AI ecosystem channels. In each channel, we have secured a lighthouse customer and have robust pipelines.' The 7 channels: 1. Hyperscalers (Oracle - delivered in 55 days vs 90-day promise) 2. Electricity providers (AEP gigawatt deal, powering AWS) 3. Gas providers (first deal signed, hyperscaler to be announced) 4. Colocation providers (Equinix 100+ MW across multiple states) 5. Neo Clouds (CoreWeave at Illinois high-performance data center) 6. Data center developers (filing permits, public records) 7. Infrastructure owners (Brookfield $5B inaugural investment) This is the most important strategic framing in any 2025 disclosure. Bloom is not just selling to AI; they have 7 different sales motions running in parallel, each with its own lighthouse and pipeline. Significantly de-risks the 'all-eggs-in-Oracle' narrative. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bar 20 Dairy biogas first
title: Bar 20 Dairy Farms (Kerman CA): Bloom's first dairy biogas project
text: Bar 20 Dairy Farms (Kerman CA): Bloom's first dairy biogas project Bloom's first dairy-farm biogas deployment was a 1 MW system at Bar 20 Dairy Farms in Kerman, California. The setup combines a methane digester, gas cleanup skid, and Bloom fuel cells. Avoids 7.7M lb CO2 / 10,500 lb NOx / 4,500 lb SO2 per MW per year vs alternatives. Biogas customers: dairies, landfills, wastewater treatment plants. Tap into low-carbon-intensity fuel incentives. Tags #bloom #customer #tech

id: 02 Atomic Notes/SOFC concentrated exhaust enables 95 percent purity carbon capture
title: Bloom's concentrated, combustion-free exhaust enables ~95% purity carbon capture cheaply
text: Bloom's concentrated, combustion-free exhaust enables ~95% purity carbon capture cheaply Because the SOFC reaction is electrochemical (no combustion), its exhaust is ~10× the CO₂ concentration of a gas turbine and free of NOx/SOx/particulates — which makes capture cheaper and lets Bloom isolate a CO₂ stream of ~95% purity for use or sequestration (Bloom carbon-capture page). The Chart Industries JV targets this against the $85/ton capture credit ($180/ton if pulled from air). Paired with biogas, the combination can deliver negative-carbon power. This is subsidy-linked terminal-value optionality, not a near-term revenue driver — alongside the SOEC electrolyzer (hydrogen), it sits in terminal value rather than the base forecast. Tags #bloom #tech #carbon-capture #optionality

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Recourse debt $2.6B Q1 2026
title: Recourse debt: $2.60B as of March 31, 2026
text: Recourse debt: $2.60B as of March 31, 2026 Bloom carries $2.60B of recourse debt on the balance sheet as of March 31, 2026 (vs $2.61B at year-end 2025). This is supported by $2.49B of cash, so net debt is roughly $100M. Debt structure includes a $2.5B convertible issued in October 2025 (zero-coupon) and other senior notes. The October 2025 refinancing simplified the cap stack but added significant share dilution potential via conversion. Tags #bloom #financials #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/AEP gigawatt deal serves AWS hyperscaler
title: AEP's 1 GW Bloom procurement is to power AWS (Amazon Web Services)
text: AEP's 1 GW Bloom procurement is to power AWS (Amazon Web Services) KR Sridhar disclosed on the Q3 2025 call (first explicit mention): 'Last year, we signed a gigawatt agreement with AEP, which purchased our fuel cell systems to power another big hyperscaler, AWS.' This is a meaningful disclosure. AEP is the utility intermediary; AWS is the actual end customer. The structure is utility-as-vendor: AEP buys from Bloom, deploys behind their meter, and sells power to AWS as a regulated rate-base asset. This is the third channel (electricity providers) in Bloom's 7-channel AI framework. Implication: AWS is already a Bloom-powered customer via AEP, even though AWS hasn't been publicly named. The AWS implied volume could be the full 1 GW. Tags #bloom #customer #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Speed to power vs gas turbine queue
title: Bloom installs in 90-120 days vs gas turbine 3-5+ year lead times
text: Bloom installs in 90-120 days vs gas turbine 3-5+ year lead times Independent sources confirm Bloom can install 50 MW in ~90 days and 100 MW in ~120 days. Gas turbine lead times (GE Vernova) are 3-5+ years. New grid interconnections take 5-10 years. Fuel cells deploy in <1 year per Goldman Sachs. Speed-to-power is the real competitive moat for AI data center primary power. Tags #bloom #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Fuel cell ITC phase-out schedule
title: Fuel cell ITC phase-out: 100% through 2033, 75% 2034, 50% 2035, 0% 2036+
text: Fuel cell ITC phase-out: 100% through 2033, 75% 2034, 50% 2035, 0% 2036+ The §48E fuel cell ITC stays at 100% through 2033, drops to 75% in 2034, 50% in 2035, and disappears in 2036. Wind and solar by contrast sunset at construction-start after July 4, 2026. Fuel cells have a vastly more durable credit runway than competing clean tech. Tags #regulation #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/100 MW per acre power density
title: Bloom power density: up to 100 MW per acre (2x typical turbine)
text: Bloom power density: up to 100 MW per acre (2x typical turbine) Bloom's installations can deliver up to 100 MW per acre, roughly double the power density of a comparable gas turbine site. The modular 325 kW blocks stack tightly without the safety setbacks turbines require. For space-constrained urban or industrial sites, this is a meaningful difference. AI data center campuses in particular are land-constrained. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Aaron Hoover hired Aug 2025 from Morgan Stanley
title: Aaron Hoover hired Aug 21, 2025 to lead Business & Corporate Development
text: Aaron Hoover hired Aug 21, 2025 to lead Business & Corporate Development Bloom hired Aaron Hoover on August 21, 2025 to head business and corporate development. He previously served as Morgan Stanley's Global Co-Head of Energy Investment Banking, one of the most senior energy IB roles on Wall Street. The hire signals Bloom is preparing for material M&A activity and/or expanded capital markets work. Pulling a sitting Global Co-Head of Energy IB in-house is not a routine corp-dev appointment. Tags #bloom #leadership #m&a #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Short interest 10 percent float Jan 2026
title: Short interest spiked to ~10% of float in early January 2026
text: Short interest spiked to ~10% of float in early January 2026 MarketBeat data shows BE short interest spiked to roughly 10% of float in early January 2026. A meaningful slice of the market is actively positioned against the stock at these multiples. Not a smoking gun on its own, but informative as a sentiment signal. Tags #bloom #financials #bear

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom cost competitive with grid in most US markets
title: Bloom is now cost-competitive with grid power in most US markets, and off-grid alternatives in nearly all markets
text: Bloom is now cost-competitive with grid power in most US markets, and off-grid alternatives in nearly all markets KR Sridhar on the Q1 call: 'Our energy servers are now cost competitive with grid power in most U.S. markets and with off-grid alternatives in nearly all markets. With over a decade of double-digit cost reductions, we remain the only on-site generation solution with a sustained downward sloping cost curve.' This directly addresses the unsubsidized LCOE comparison in our existing tax credit analysis. Even before the §48E ITC, Bloom claims grid parity in most US markets. The cost curve goes down annually while gas turbines have flat to rising cost curves. Tags #bloom #financials #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/2026 risk factors from earnings release
title: Q1 2026 release-disclosed risk factors: 21 items including OBBBA, AI slowdown, FEOC
text: Q1 2026 release-disclosed risk factors: 21 items including OBBBA, AI slowdown, FEOC Bloom's Q1 2026 release lists 21 explicit risk factors. Notable inclusions for analyst tracking: (1) emerging distributed-generation market; (4) ability to service debt; (10) availability of tax credits; (11) impact of IRA and OBBBA; (15) trade policies including tariffs; (18) actual or perceived slowdown in AI adoption; (20) ability of backlog to convert to revenue. Number 18 (AI slowdown) and number 11 (regulatory) are the two macro risks worth pricing actively. Tags #bloom #bear #regulation

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q4 2025 product margin 37 percent vs Q1 2026 35
title: Q4 2025 product gross margin 37% (peak); Q1 2026 settled at 35.3%
text: Q4 2025 product gross margin 37% (peak); Q1 2026 settled at 35.3% Q4 2025 product gross margin reached approximately 37% non-GAAP. Q1 2026 came in at 35.3%, a modest sequential decline. Management attributes the variance to project mix. For reference: FY2026 non-GAAP gross margin guidance is ~34%, suggesting management is conservatively assuming continued mix volatility rather than peak-quarter rates. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/99.999 percent uptime 5 nines
title: Bloom claims 99.999% availability (five-nines) at multi-MW scale
text: Bloom claims 99.999% availability (five-nines) at multi-MW scale Bloom's data center marketing claims 99.9% to 99.999% (five-nines) availability for Series 10 systems up to 10 MW. Five-nines equates to roughly 5 minutes of unplanned downtime per year. This is Bloom's stated number; no independent third-party audit confirms it. Worth treating as a marketing claim until verified. Compare to typical utility grid availability (~99.9% / three-nines). Tags #bloom #tech #moat #needs-verification

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Initial 2026 guidance subsequently raised in Q1
title: Initial 2026 guidance (Feb 5, 2026): $3.1-3.3B revenue, 32% gross margin; RAISED in Q1 to $3.4-3.8B, 34%
text: Initial 2026 guidance (Feb 5, 2026): $3.1-3.3B revenue, 32% gross margin; RAISED in Q1 to $3.4-3.8B, 34% On the Q4 2025 call (Feb 5, 2026), Maciej Kurzymski gave initial 2026 guidance: revenue $3.1-3.3B, non-GAAP gross margin ~32%, non-GAAP operating income $125-475M, capex $150-200M, cash flow from ops ~$200M. On the Q1 2026 call (Apr 28, 2026), management raised the revenue range to $3.4-3.8B (lower end above prior upper bound), gross margin to ~34%, operating income to $600-750M. The Q1 raise was a roughly $400M revenue lift and $150M+ operating income lift in just one quarter. This is a management credibility data point worth tracking: the initial guide was raised materially within 12 weeks. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Carbon capture European interest near zero on natural gas
title: Strong European interest in Bloom's carbon capture solution for near-net-zero on natural gas
text: Strong European interest in Bloom's carbon capture solution for near-net-zero on natural gas KR Sridhar (Q3 2025): 'And the interest in Europe for our carbon capture solutions where you can go to almost net zero using natural gas as a fuel - tremendous interest there. No other technology, the turbines and the engines cannot do that. We can. So tremendous interest there.' This ties to the Bloom-Chart Industries partnership for §45Q. Europe doesn't have §45Q but has carbon pricing via EU ETS and aggressive corporate net-zero commitments. SOFC's concentrated CO2 exhaust gives Bloom a near-zero-carbon-on-gas option that turbines structurally cannot match. Tags #bloom #tech #regulation #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Near-zero NOx SOx no combustion
title: Near-zero NOx and SOx emissions because there is no combustion
text: Near-zero NOx and SOx emissions because there is no combustion Because the Bloom SOFC converts fuel electrochemically rather than burning it, NOx and SOx emissions are essentially eliminated. This matters specifically for siting in non-attainment air-quality regions (California, Texas, parts of the Northeast) where gas turbines require complex permitting or aren't permitted at all. It also matters for the carbon-capture story: cleaner exhaust is cheaper to capture from. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Community acceptance gating factor for AI infrastructure
title: Community acceptance is becoming the gating factor for AI infrastructure permitting
text: Community acceptance is becoming the gating factor for AI infrastructure permitting KR Sridhar argued community acceptance is now the binding constraint on AI data center buildout: 'As permits and permissions become the gating factor for AI infrastructure, community acceptance matters increasingly.' Bloom's compact footprint, no-combustion exhaust, minimal water use, and quiet operation are positioned as the answer. Gas turbines lose this argument structurally; the noise, water, air quality, and electricity-rate impacts are well documented. This is why Project Jupiter pivoted: BorderPlex was facing local opposition to the turbine plan. Tags #bloom #moat #customer

id: 02 Atomic Notes/CFO search urgency no rush Q3 2025
title: CFO search status at Q3 2025: 'sense of urgency but no sense of rush' (Simon Edwards hired ~April 2026)
text: CFO search status at Q3 2025: 'sense of urgency but no sense of rush' (Simon Edwards hired ~April 2026) Colin Rusch (Oppenheimer) asked about the CFO search on the Q3 2025 call (November 2025). KR: 'Look, it is a very important position for us. We take it very seriously. So we have a process in place. And that search is going on. We have a sense of urgency. But no sense of rush. We will let you know when we hire one.' Timeline: search ongoing Q3 2025 → Maciej Kurzymski acting at Q4 2025 call (Feb 2026) → Simon Edwards confirmed by Q1 2026 call (April 28, 2026). So the search took roughly 4-6 months from the Q3 acknowledgment. For any analyst, the CFO interregnum is a governance signal worth noting but not concerning given KR's framing and Simon's arrival in time for guidance season. Tags #bloom #leadership

id: 02 Atomic Notes/3 financing models CapEx PPA Managed Services
title: Bloom has 3 go-to-market financing models: CapEx (direct), PPA, and Managed Services (legacy/wind-down)
text: Bloom has 3 go-to-market financing models: CapEx (direct), PPA, and Managed Services (legacy/wind-down) Maciej Kurzymski explained the financing structures on the Q3 2025 call: 1. CapEx (direct sale) : Customer writes a big check, effectively prepaying for years of electricity. Some quarter-to-quarter activity. 2. PPA financing : The majority of transactions. Bloom or partner finances; customer pays per kWh. 3. Managed Services (sale-leaseback) : 'We have not done managed service transactions in quite some time. We do not expect to do those going forward.' The explicit retirement of Managed Services confirms Bloom's strategic exit from the Electricity-segment own-the-box model. Going forward = mostly PPA + some CapEx. This matters for revenue mix forecasting. Tags #bloom #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/AEP 1 GW $2.65B procurement
title: AEP 1 GW procurement agreement, ~$2.65B
text: AEP 1 GW procurement agreement, ~$2.65B November 2025: Bloom signed a gigawatt fuel cell procurement agreement with American Electric Power, up to ~$2.65B. AEP serves AI data centers across its territory. Largest commercial fuel cell procurement at time of signing. Tags #bloom #customer #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/5-year non-GAAP operating income $-38M to $221M
title: Non-GAAP operating income: -$38M (21) → $221M (25) → $600-750M (26E)
text: Non-GAAP operating income: -$38M (21) → $221M (25) → $600-750M (26E) Bloom went from a $38M non-GAAP operating loss in 2021 to a $221M profit in 2025, then guides to $600-750M in 2026. text: 2025 factory utilization was ~39%; a new-factory decision is projected for mid-to-late 2027 Per Kshana research ( estimate ): Bloom's 2025 manufacturing utilization was roughly 39% , tightening fast on the back of Q1 2026's +130% revenue. It adds capacity on a continuous "analog dial" (hundreds of MW/quarter) toward a 2 GW internal stack target (end-2026) , with the existing footprint stretchable to 5 GW . A greenfield facility is only triggered once demand consistently exceeds the 5 GW ceiling — projected mid-to-late 2027 (to be ready before the 2029–2030 demand peak, given ~12–18-month "copy exact" lead times). Inventory was front-loaded to ~$643M at FY2025 end. ⚠ Utilization % and the 2027 trigger are estimates/projections, not disclosed. Directionally consistent with our verified "5 GW is the ceiling, not current output" finding. Tags #bloom #manufacturing #capacity

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom microgrid no grid no diesel no battery no turbine
title: Bloom's microgrid model uses no grid, no diesel, no batteries, no turbines (just SOFC + ultracapacitor)
text: Bloom's microgrid model uses no grid, no diesel, no batteries, no turbines (just SOFC + ultracapacitor) KR Sridhar described the standard hyperscaler deployment: 'No grid, no dirty diesel generators for backup, no battery banks for load following, no engines, no turbines, just Bloom and Bloom alone.' Load following is handled by ultracapacitors built into the Bloom system, eliminating the need for separate battery storage. This is structurally important: it eliminates the bear argument that data centers will pair fuel cells with batteries (and pay extra for the battery cost). Bloom's combined SOFC + ultracapacitor solution is self-sufficient. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

text: Oracle strategic partnership warrants priced at MARKET (not penny warrants) Sherif Elmaghrabi (BTIG) asked about the warrant transaction with Oracle. KR Sridhar: 'Remember, these are not penny warrants. These were done at market pricing on the day we agreed to it. So it is not in lieu of something other than both parties enhancing enterprise value.' This is important because penny warrants would have been heavily dilutive value transfer to Oracle. Market-priced warrants are a much more conventional strategic-partner alignment mechanism. Similar transactions with other hyperscalers are possible but case-by-case. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Copy exact model for new factories
title: Bloom's 'copy exact' model lets them build new factories faster than any peer
text: Bloom's 'copy exact' model lets them build new factories faster than any peer Beyond the current 5 GW footprint, KR Sridhar said: 'Our supply chain and manufacturing strategy and planning allows us to build that capacity significantly faster than any other option in the market using our copy exact model. We will strive to bring power to our customers faster than they can stand up their greenfield facilities.' 'Copy exact' is an Intel-pioneered manufacturing concept: replicate an existing factory's processes exactly to minimize ramp risk. Bloom appears to be using this approach to compress new-factory commissioning timelines. Tags #bloom #moat #tech

id: 02 Atomic Notes/54 percent lifetime electrical efficiency on gas
title: 54% is Bloom's honest lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas
text: 54% is Bloom's honest lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas Three efficiency figures circulate and measure different things; the honest, real-world number on gas is 54% lifetime average electrical efficiency (degradation included), per Bloom's own Heat Capture brochure [R]. 54% — lifetime average electrical efficiency on natural gas 60% — electrical efficiency on 100% hydrogen at the R&D facility 90% — combined electricity plus captured, used heat (CHP) — not the electrical figure, and not applicable to data centers unless the heat is used The trap is stacking 90% against 54%. On gas with the heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. (The datasheet's 53–65% is the Server 6.5 nameplate range; 54% is the de-rated lifetime figure.) At 54%, the box needs 6.32 MMBtu of gas per MWh. Tags #bloom #tech #efficiency

text: Related-party revenue exploded from $2.8M (Q1'25) to $574.2M (Q4'25) to $373.3M (Q1'26) The Brookfield partnership took related-party revenue from a rounding error to ~half the top line in four quarters: Q1 2025 $2.8M (0.86% of revenue) → Q4 2025 $574.2M → Q1 2026 $373.3M (49.7% of the record $751.1M) . Context: SK ecoplant stopped being a related party on July 10, 2025, so post-2025 related-party revenue is essentially the Brookfield Fund JVs. This trajectory is the quantitative core of the revenue-quality debate. ✓ CONFIRMED — the $2.8M, $574.2M, and $373.3M/49.7% figures reconcile with SEC filings (Q1 2026 release / FY2025 10-K). id: 02 Atomic Notes/SOEC reverse mode 80 percent hydrogen efficiency
title: SOEC reverse mode hits ~80% electrical-to-hydrogen efficiency with waste heat
text: SOEC reverse mode hits ~80% electrical-to-hydrogen efficiency with waste heat Bloom's solid oxide electrolyzer runs the same hardware in reverse: electricity + water → hydrogen. When paired with industrial waste heat the efficiency reaches ~80% electrical-to-hydrogen, which Bloom claims is the highest on the market. Why it matters: best-in-class efficiency is the difference between hitting the §45V $3/kg top tier vs. lower tiers. Hardware is the same as the power-generation unit. Tags #bloom #tech #regulation

text: Product cost fell an estimated ~$3,850/kW (2021) to ~$2,275/kW (2026) — validating double-digit cost-downs Per Kshana research ( reconstructed estimate , not disclosed): Bloom's product cost per kW descended from roughly $3,850/kW (2021) to a projected ~$2,275/kW (2026) — a ~41% cumulative drop that supports the company's claim of ≥10% annual cost reductions , even through the 2022–2023 inflationary bump. Gross margin reportedly expanded ~650 bps while absorbing a ~100 bps tariff headwind in early 2025. This is the concrete answer to "how real is the cost-down curve," and it's the mechanism by which Bloom approaches grid parity and can absorb lower Brookfield-JV pricing. But per-kW cost is proprietary "edge" data — treat the exact figures as directional, not audited. ⚠ Estimate; Bloom does not disclose $/kW cost.
