id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom reactive power range advantage over turbines
title: Bloom delivers reactive power across a much wider range than turbines/engines (grid stabilization advantage)
text: Bloom delivers reactive power across a much wider range than turbines/engines (grid stabilization advantage) KR Sridhar explained an under-appreciated Bloom advantage: 'Engines and turbines can offer reactive power... only in a very narrow range. Bloom Energy Corporation has an amazing range in terms of that power factor. And that benefit will be enormous in places like PJM, where you have the condition and the grid instability. Or places like California where the amount of renewables you have completely destabilize the grid. We are a stabilizing factor.' Reactive power supports grid voltage and is increasingly scarce as renewables (which don't provide it) grow. Bloom can sell this as an ancillary service to utilities. Also useful as a regulatory argument: Bloom installations help fix grid problems rather than create them. Tags #bloom #tech #moat #regulation

id: 02 Atomic Notes/10-15 year figure is service commitment not stack life
title: The "10–15 years" figure is the service-commitment length, not the physical stack life
text: The "10–15 years" figure is the service-commitment length, not the physical stack life Correction to a common conflation (the Kshana research made it): the 10–15 years (up to 20) cited for data-center deals is the service-contract commitment , not how long a fuel-cell stack physically lasts. Actual stack life is ~4.8–5.2 years for 2016–18 vintages — improving ~2.5× vs first-generation — and stacks are swapped on-site in under an hour when they degrade. Over a 10–20-year contract a customer therefore pays for multiple stack replacements , which is exactly what makes service a recurring annuity. So "10–15-year stack life" (Kshana) is misleading; the accurate read is a 10–20-year service relationship built on ~5-year replaceable stacks. The bull point still holds — steady data-center duty is easier on stacks than cycling, and service margin has inflected positive — but the long number is a contract term, not a durability spec. ✓ CONFIRMED via Bloom longevity disclosures (4.8/5.2-yr vintages, 2.5× improvement, <1-hr swap; 10–20-yr service commitments). Sources: Bloom – longevity Tags #bloom #service #stack-life #correction

text: Bloom Server is fuel-flexible: NG, biogas, hydrogen (~91% NG today) The same Bloom hardware runs on natural gas, biogas, or pure hydrogen with no mechanical changes. Ammonia and methanol mentioned as future fuels. In practice, ~91% of installed capacity today runs on standard natural gas from the existing pipeline network. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom Server 6.5 efficiency 53-65 percent
title: Bloom Server 6.5 efficiency: 53–65% LHV electrical
text: Bloom Server 6.5 efficiency: 53–65% LHV electrical The current Bloom Energy Server 6.5 specifies 53% to 65% electrical efficiency on a Lower Heating Value, net AC basis. The 65% top of range is reached only at steady partial-load operation; 53% is closer to lifetime average. Equivalent heat rate is 5,811 to 7,127 BTU/kWh. Why it matters: this is the headline efficiency claim that justifies the price premium over the grid and over gas peakers. Roughly two-thirds of input fuel becomes electricity. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/SOFC operates above 800C
title: Bloom SOFC core operating temperature above 800°C
text: Bloom SOFC core operating temperature above 800°C The Bloom Server operates at a core temperature above 800°C. This high temperature enables internal reforming of natural gas to hydrogen without a separate reformer, removes the need for precious-metal catalysts, and makes SOFC the most sulfur-tolerant fuel cell chemistry. The double-edged sword: high temperature drives both the efficiency advantage AND the ceramic stack degradation that requires periodic replacement. Tags #bloom #tech

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Nuclear SMR objection timeline mismatch
title: Nuclear/SMR is not direct competition because of timeline mismatch
text: Nuclear/SMR is not direct competition because of timeline mismatch Small modular reactors (SMRs) remain largely a 2030s technology, and conventional reactors still take ~10 years to permit and build. That is exactly the bottleneck driving data centers to Bloom in the first place. Bloom is the answer for power needed in 1-2 years; nuclear is potentially the answer for baseload needed 10 years out. The two can coexist for a long time. The more important question is the terminal-value one: does on-site distributed generation become a permanent layer, or does nuclear/grid eventually displace it? Tags #bloom #moat #competitor

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Scandia-stabilized zirconia scandium most FEOC-sensitive input
title: Scandia-stabilized zirconia (scandium) is Bloom's most FEOC-sensitive input
text: Scandia-stabilized zirconia (scandium) is Bloom's most FEOC-sensitive input Per Kshana research (verify): Bloom's SOFC electrolyte uses Scandia-Stabilized Zirconia (ScSZ) / scandia-ceria-stabilized zirconia for higher conductivity at lower temperature — making scandium the most FEOC-sensitive material in the bill of materials. A large share of global scandium historically comes from Soviet-era stockpiles or is processed in China (a "covered nation"), so even with domestic final assembly, the mineral's upstream "material assistance" can jeopardize customers' 30% ITC. The regulatory framework is confirmed (OBBB + Bipartisan Policy Center analysis): the Material Assistance Cost Ratio (MACR) tests the % of component value sourced from non-Prohibited Foreign Entities (must exceed a threshold to qualify). A "Foreign-Influenced Entity" is triggered at 25% ownership / a board seat / 40% aggregate / 15% of debt. IRS safe-harbor tables are due end-2026 ; Treasury updates critical-mineral thresholds by Dec 31, 2027 ; contracts finalized before June 16, 2025 (in service before Jan 1, 2030) are grandfathered. ◑ PARTIAL: the FEOC/MACR framework and dates are confirmed ( Bipartisan Policy Center · DOE FEOC guidance ). Bloom's specific scandium sourcing and its measured MACR are NOT publicly confirmed — the load-bearing unknown; verify via filings/expert channels. Tags #bloom #feoc #supply-chain #risk

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom CHP boosts efficiency to 90 percent
title: Combined Heat and Power pushes total system efficiency above 90%
text: Combined Heat and Power pushes total system efficiency above 90% Bloom's Integrated Heat Capture / CHP product captures the 800C+ exhaust heat and reuses it for hot water, building heat, process steam, or chilled water via absorption chillers. Combined electrical + thermal efficiency rises from ~54% (electricity only) to over 90%. Why it matters: in data center deployments, absorption-chiller cooling can drive a 10-15% PUE improvement, turning the worst-case loss (wasted heat) into a second product. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom only solution natively producing 800V DC today
title: Bloom is the only on-site power solution that natively produces 800V DC today
text: Bloom is the only on-site power solution that natively produces 800V DC today KR Sridhar: 'Bloom, and only Bloom, natively produces 800 volts DC today. No Band-Aids, or adapters needed.' Gas turbines, reciprocating engines, and the grid all deliver AC at high voltage (35-69 kV). To serve an 800V DC AI rack, they require transformers, rectifiers, and power conditioning that add cost, reduce reliability, and increase losses. Bloom's solid-state architecture skips all of this. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/FERC 60-day data center interconnection rule proposed Q3 2025
title: DOE Energy Secretary proposed FERC rule limiting data center interconnection review to 60 days
text: DOE Energy Secretary proposed FERC rule limiting data center interconnection review to 60 days Manav Gupta (UBS) raised on the Q3 2025 call: 'Last week, Energy Secretary sent a draft proposal to FERC that would limit the regulatory review period for data center connections to power grid to just 60 days. Expediting a process that can currently extend up to years.' KR Sridhar's response: 'We applaud the policymakers and regulators for doing that.' But importantly, the announcement explicitly states data centers 'are entering the age of BYOP, bring your own power. You get curtailed even if you have an interconnection if you do not bring your own power.' Net for Bloom: even faster grid interconnection still requires BYOP capability. The proposed rule reinforces Bloom's positioning rather than undermines it. Tags #bloom #regulation #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Q1 2026 130 percent growth is 17 percent ex-affiliate
title: Q1 2026's reported 130% growth is ~17% once the affiliate channel is stripped out
text: Q1 2026's reported 130% growth is ~17% once the affiliate channel is stripped out The headline Q1 2026 revenue growth of ~130% collapses to ~17% when the Brookfield/SK affiliate (Fund-JV) channel is removed. The quarter split roughly in half: $377.8M arm's-length (+16.9% YoY) vs $373.3M affiliate [D]/[R]. The two channels grow on different logic — arm's-length answers to market demand and factory capacity; the affiliate channel answers to Brookfield's capital-deployment pace — so they must be modeled as separate stacks and summed. Mashing them together is how a 130% quarter reads as a 17% one. The affiliate channel also carries an equity-method loss and a deferred-profit contra that scale with affiliate shipments. Tags #bloom #financials #affiliate #risk

text: $2.5B of 0% convertible notes due 2030 (Nov 2025) refinanced $976M of 3% 2028/29 debt Settled Nov 4, 2025 : $2.5B of 0%-coupon convertible senior notes due Nov 15, 2030 (upsized from $1.75B). id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bloom DC architecture since 2000 every server 15 years
title: Bloom's DC architecture has shipped in every server for the last 15 years
text: Bloom's DC architecture has shipped in every server for the last 15 years KR Sridhar: 'We built an architecture where we can feed these straws appropriately right at that 800 volts and we decided every unit we have shipped for the last 15 years has that. But after that, we have one other box that takes that DC and makes it into AC and provides it because we were making color TV images. The world was only consuming black and white.' The 'color TV / black and white' analogy: Bloom has been generating native DC and converting it to AC for the AC-only legacy data center market. Going forward, Bloom can simply remove the DC-to-AC inverter and ship native 800V DC. The capability has been there for 15 years and is now finally what the market wants. For retrofit potential: previously shipped servers can be modified to remove the inverter, so Bloom's existing installed base is forward-compatible. Tags #bloom #tech #moat

id: 02 Atomic Notes/All-in cost 121 per MWh fuel slice small
title: Bloom's all-in cost is ~$121/MWh (~$95 with the 30% ITC); fuel is only $22–32 of it
text: Bloom's all-in cost is ~$121/MWh (~$95 with the 30% ITC); fuel is only $22–32 of it Bloom's levelized all-in delivered cost is roughly $121/MWh unsubsidized , or ~$95/MWh with the 30% ITC [D]. The fuel slice is only $22–32/MWh (6.32 MMBtu/MWh at 54% efficiency × $3.50–5.00/MMBtu); the box and stack-replacement service are the other four-fifths. Implication: higher efficiency shrinks only the fuel slice. Bloom undercuts an inefficient on-site genset on fuel (a 40% genset burns 8.53 MMBtu/MWh) yet still costs more than grid power overall. Bloom sells speed and reliability, not commodity price. Hydrogen makes the fuel line ~10× worse (~$200/MWh at $4–6/kg). Tags #bloom #cost #competition #fuel

id: 02 Atomic Notes/OBBBA fuel cell ITC carve-out
title: OBBBA §48E: flat 30% ITC for fuel cells, no PWA, no GHG test
text: OBBBA §48E: flat 30% ITC for fuel cells, no PWA, no GHG test The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (signed July 4, 2025) moved fuel cells into the technology-neutral §48E ITC at a flat 30% credit. Fuel cells were carved out of the prevailing-wage/apprenticeship requirement AND from the zero-GHG test, so natural-gas SOFC qualifies for the full 30%. The single most important regulatory development for Bloom in 2025. Tags #regulation #moat #financials

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Hindenburg 2019 short report
title: Hindenburg Sept 2019: efficiency 58.3% → 51% in 25 months, stack life 4.8-5.2 yrs
text: Hindenburg Sept 2019: efficiency 58.3% → 51% in 25 months, stack life 4.8-5.2 yrs Hindenburg Research's September 2019 short report argued Bloom's economics were materially worse than disclosed. Key claims: real-world efficiency degraded from 58.3% at install to ~51% after 25 months; average stack life was 4.8-5.2 years against >5-year warranties, meaning service costs were structurally underreserved. Bloom disputed the report, but the underlying physics (high-temp ceramic stacks degrade) is real. Same 'double-edged sword' as section 1 of the report. Tags #bloom #bear

id: 05 Questions/Stack life under heavier AI duty cycles
title: Will stack life hold up under heavier AI data center duty cycles?
text: Will stack life hold up under heavier AI data center duty cycles? Why this matters Historical Bloom MTTR data (1.9 → 4.7 years) is from commercial customers with relatively steady loads. AI data centers run at much higher utilization (~70-85% capacity factor sustained) than typical commercial use. Faster degradation under harder duty cycles could break the unit economics and the Service segment math. What I have so far Hindenburg 2019 short report Bloom MTTR 1.9 to 4.7 years What I still need Bloom field data from any high-utilization deployments (Equinix, Apple) Independent academic studies on SOFC degradation vs duty cycle Service margin disclosure as installed base grows

id: 05 Questions/Open Questions — Answered June 2026
title: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, June 2026)
text: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, June 2026) Web research answering the eleven open questions. Each entry: verdict , key evidence, what stays non-public, and sources. Confidence noted. Academic use; not investment advice. Q1 · Pricing discipline when gas turbines catch up (2028–2029)? Verdict (med-high): The squeeze is real but back-half-decade, not imminent. Turbine lead times are near their ~5-year peak now and only begin compressing around 2027, with real relief 2029–2030. Bloom's speed (12–18-mo deploy), native DC, and double-digit annual cost-downs should hold pricing through ~2028; risk builds 2029–2030 as turbine surplus and falling turbine prices arrive together. GE Vernova ~80 GW backlog, effectively sold out through 2030; Siemens Energy €136B backlog; Mitsubishi turbines ordered now deliver 2028–2030. Jefferies: equipment capacity 19 GW (2028) → 49 (2029) → 76 (2030); lead times "start dropping by 2027." Turbine prices +195% since 2019 (~$600/kW) — currently helping Bloom. _Non-public:_ Bloom's per-kW COGS trajectory; confidential turbine ASPs. Sources: Utility Dive – GE Vernova · Utility Dive – bottleneck easing · Wood Mackenzie – turbine prices Q4 · On-site power: permanent layer or temporary bridge? Verdict (med): Hyperscalers explicitly call gas/fuel cells a "bridge" to grid + nuclear — but the bridge runs well into the 2030s and the endpoint is undefined, so on-site DG is effectively a structural layer for this decade and likely persists as permanent resilience/peaking capacity. SMRs realistically 2028–2030 for first small units, bulk 2031–2035 (Kairos/Google ~2030; Oklo ~2027–28, microreactor scale). Grid interconnection waits average ~5 years; FERC Order 2023 + June 2026 large-load orders aim to help but near-term relief doubted. Microsoft LOI 1.4 GW behind-the-meter gas; Oracle hedges Bloom SOFC + VoltaGrid gas. _Non-public:_ how much on-site capacity hyperscalers retire once grid/nuclear arrive. Sources: Latitude Media · FERC RM26-4 · WWT – SMRs Q11 · Is the 800V DC transition real, and is Bloom ahead? Verdict (high on direction): Real, physics-driven, NVIDIA-forced. Pilots 2026, broad adoption 2027–2028 with the Kyber/Rubin Ultra generation. Bloom is genuinely ahead — its cells natively output DC and (per Bloom) no rival natively produces 800V DC today — but it's a first-mover edge, not a patent moat: AC→800VDC "sidecars" (Vertiv, Schneider) let turbine/grid power reach 800V racks too. NVIDIA standardizing 800 VDC, first in Kyber racks (2027, ~600 kW/rack); moves >150% more power through the same copper. Vertiv 800V systems shipping H2 2026. Adoption favors greenfield builds (brownfield AC retrofit costly) — good for Bloom's new-build pairing. UBS raised PT $170→$251 on 800VDC readiness. _Non-public:_ system-level efficiency gain of native-DC vs sidecar; whether hyperscalers pay a premium for native DC. Sources: NVIDIA developer blog · SemiAnalysis · DCD – 1MW racks Q6 · Why is installation gross margin still ~−35%? Verdict: A small, lumpy, non-strategic line — a mix/scale artifact, not a deliberate loss-leader. Install is <4% of revenue; the −35% reflects a collapsing install-revenue base against relatively fixed costs as more installs move to Brookfield-/customer-financed EPC. Q1'26 install revenue $25.9M vs cost $35.1M = −$9.1M (−35.3%). Trend: Q1'25 +1.0% → Q4'25 −10.7% → Q1'26 −35.3%. Product margin 34.3%, service +13.3%, consolidated 30.0% — install drag immaterial. Industrial EPC/install typically low-single-digit to ~10% GM, so structurally negative isn't the norm; it's Bloom's portfolio choice. No explicit "break-even at scale" guidance. Sources: Q1 2026 results (SEC) · Q1 2026 transcript Q7 · Can margins survive a hyperscaler renegotiation? Verdict (med): Deals are multi-year master agreements with volume frameworks, but product largely ships order-by-order off those frameworks, so pricing is periodically reset, not locked for life. Bloom keeps more pricing power than a commodity supplier (sole-source, grid-independent, 100%-attached service annuity), but concentration risk is real and rising. Oracle: MSA up to 2.8 GW (only ~1.2 GW contracted) — a ceiling, not full take-or-pay; Oracle got a ~$400M Bloom warrant (Bloom conceded economics). AEP: $2.65B / 20-yr / ~1 GW (closest to locked). SK: historically take-or-pay with "premium pricing." Top-3 customers ≈ 68% of FY2025 revenue (43/13/12), largest is related-party. _Non-public:_ per-watt pricing, MFN/cancellation clauses, gross margin by customer cohort. Sources: CNBC – Oracle/warrant · FY2025 10-K (SEC) Q9 · Real quality of revenue given ~50% related-party? Verdict (high on facts): Bloom states Brookfield-JV sales are "transacted at arms-length and prevailing market terms" and it defers intra-entity profit — so revenue isn't obviously inflated. But quality is muddied: ~44–50% routes through JVs Bloom co-owns and finances, and Bloom books equity-method losses on them. Q1'26: related-party $373.3M vs $377.8M arm's-length. FY2025: $892.0M of $2,024.0M ≈ 44%. Pricing = "cost-plus margin." Equity-method loss −$17.0M Q1'26 (−$40.4M FY2025); deferred-profit liability $22.8M. SK ecoplant ceased to be a related party July 10, 2025 — so related-party is now essentially the Brookfield Fund JVs. _Non-public:_ gross-margin split related vs third-party; end-customer economics inside the JVs; independent verification of "market terms." This is the legitimate red flag to discount for. Sources: Q1 2026 results (SEC) · FY2025 10-K (SEC) Q8 · Will stack life hold under heavy AI duty cycles? Verdict (med-high on direction): Continuous, steady-state data-center loads are likely neutral-to-favorable vs the on/off cycling that historically drives SOFC degradation — which structurally supports the thesis. Real risk is elevated current density at near-100% utilization, and that Bloom's multi-decade fade at data-center duty is still unproven at scale. Literature: thermal cycling dominates degradation (voltage decay 8.8% at 1°C/min vs 19.1% at 5°C/min); higher current density compounds fade. Bloom MTTR improved 1.9 yrs (2011) → 4.7 yrs (2015); median life 4.9 yrs, some >7.7. Hindenburg 2019 alleged 58.3%→51% fade in 25 months; warranties ~95% output / ~52% efficiency. Service gross margin inflected: Q4'25 +16.9% (vs −1.7% yr earlier), 6th straight profitable quarter — the annuity is starting to pay. _Non-public:_ actual current-density set-points, per-vintage fade curves, stack-replacement cost per MWh. Sources: ACS – thermal cycling · Bloom – longevity · Hindenburg 2019 Q10 · What if FEOC / material-assistance compliance fails? Verdict (med): The regime is now concrete (IRS Notice 2026-15, Feb 2026) and governs the §48E credit Bloom's customers rely on. Bloom's near-fully-domestic US manufacturing (Fremont, Newark) positions it comparatively well, but FEOC-vulnerable inputs (scandia/yttria-stabilized zirconia electrolyte, nickel, power electronics — often China-linked) could breach the cost-ratio test and strip a customer's 30% ITC. OBBBA added FEOC/"material assistance" cost-ratio tests to §48E/§45Y/§45X, effective tax years after 2025. Notice 2026-15 gives interim safe harbors; broader PFE-status guidance deferred (long-tail audit risk). _Non-public (load-bearing):_ Bloom's exact bill-of-materials sourcing and its measured material-assistance cost ratio — the unknown that decides customer-ITC survival. Sources: IRS – material assistance guidance · Novogradac – Notice 2026-15 Q2 · Will biogas and hydrogen ever scale for Bloom? Verdict (med-high): Unlikely to shift the mix materially this decade. The fleet is ~91% natural gas, the AI boom pulls Bloom further toward bankable gas baseload, and both alternatives face ceilings — RNG supply is small and transport-dominated, and green hydrogen's §45V lifeline now needs construction start before Jan 1, 2028. N. American RNG ~71.5M MMBtu (2022) → ~160M by 2030, mostly for transport. §45V up to $3/kg (LCOH >$4 → <$1.50) but ~52 clean-H₂ projects cancelled in 18 months to Sept 2025. No disclosed quantified 2030 fuel-mix target; demand signal is all gas ($5B Brookfield, $7.65B of DC contracts in a 90-day window). Sources: cCarbon – RNG outlook · 45V 2028 deadline Q5 · How durable is the 30% ITC after the 2033 phase-out? Verdict (high on schedule): Under OBBBA (July 2025), §48E(j) gives fuel cells a flat 30% rate (no prevailing-wage, no adders, no emissions test), but the credit steps down by construction-start year: 100% through 2033, 75% (2034), 50% (2035), 0% (2036+). So it's durable and generous through end-2033, then sunsets. Given wind/solar precedent of constant revision, treat 2034+ as soft — but that tail sits far outside a 2026–2028 base case. Transferability (§6418) preserved; FEOC/material-assistance rules apply. Bloom claims ITC at placed-in-service/COD , not construction-start; backlog value "reflects anticipated ITC." Lobbying: FCHEA is lead advocate (helped restore §45V + fuel-cell ITC in OBBBA). Note: the "ITC expiring/Congressional inaction" alarm circulating online is dated July 2024, pre-OBBBA — not the current state. Sources: Project Finance – §48E fuel cells · RSM – OBBBA clean energy · ITC/PTC history Q3 · When must Bloom break ground on a new factory beyond 5 GW? Verdict (high): Not near-term — and the premise needs reframing. ~5 GW is not Bloom's current output; it's the expandable ceiling of the existing Fremont + Newark buildings under "copy exact." Run-rate is ~1 GW today → ~2 GW by end-2026, with headroom to ~5 GW inside existing shells at ~$100M per incremental GW. A greenfield factory is only forced if demand sustainably exceeds ~5 GW — plausibly a 2028+ question. Math: 5 GW × ~$3,000–4,000/kW ≈ $15–20B of product revenue — the existing footprint alone can roughly service the entire ~$20B backlog. 2026 is delivery/COD-paced, not capacity-constrained (guide $3.4–3.8B). "Copy exact" replication ~12–18 months; a new building would be US-sited for FEOC/IRA reasons. No new-factory site/timing announced as of Q1 2026. _Non-public:_ exact ASP, current utilization, per-site COD schedule. Sources: Utility Dive – 2 GW capacity · Fremont factory opening What this changes for the thesis The bull case hinges on the turbine bottleneck (Q1) and grid/nuclear lag (Q4) keeping speed-to-power valuable long enough for the native 800V DC edge (Q11) to convert into durable backlog — with the 2028–2030 window as the key bear pivot when turbine supply, SMR pilots, and AC-to-800VDC sidecars could erode Bloom's advantages at once. The most durable open risk is revenue quality (Q9) : ~half of sales route through JVs Bloom finances, and the related-vs-third-party margin split is simply not disclosed.

id: 05 Questions/Open Questions — Answered July 2026
title: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, July 2026)
text: Open Questions — Answered (research pass, July 2026) Follow-up pass on the four questions opened by the June 30 Brookfield expansion and the July 8 Hunterbrook short report. Format matches the June synthesis: verdict, evidence, what stays non-public, sources. Q1 · Is Bloom's scandium supply really China-dependent, and does the scarcity math hold? Verdict (med — genuinely contested): Both sides are partially right. Hunterbrook's current-market facts are stronger than Bloom's denial suggests; Bloom's potential-supply argument is stronger than Hunterbrook's scarcity math suggests. The scarcity numbers: independent data (INN/market sources) put current global scandium oxide production at only ~25–40 t/yr against ~117 t/yr projected demand — so at today's production, even Hunterbrook's "240 t projected supply" is generous. If 5 GW really needs ~220 t/yr, supply must scale several-fold regardless of geography . Bloom's counter: scandium is an economics problem, not an availability problem — it's more abundant in the crust than lead, and titanium-processing waste streams (10M t of ore/yr, >half processed outside China) could yield "several hundred tons" annually via Bloom's proprietary tailings-recovery processes. The China question: Hunterbrook's most concrete claims — a Hunan Oriental Scandium sales rep calling itself "BE's largest supplier" (Hunan claims >50% of global fuel-cell-grade share), and trade data showing Hong Kong Three Circle Electronic shipping "ceramic substrate for fuel cell application" to Bloom in 2024–25 — were not specifically refuted. Bloom's blog (posted Jul 7, updated Jul 8 — the day of the report ) says it sources from "multiple sources and suppliers across multiple countries," is "not dependent on China," and keeps sourcing confidential. Note the careful wording: not dependent ≠ not sourcing from China today. New wrinkle: a second short attack ("Crossroads Capital") followed Hunterbrook; Bloom denied again and the stock closed higher. _Non-public (load-bearing):_ Bloom's actual supplier list and volumes; whether Chinese material is in the current mix even if substitutable. Note this also feeds the FEOC/§48E material-assistance question (June Q10) — if Chinese scandium is in the BOM, it's not just a supply risk, it's a customer-ITC risk. For the presentation: frame as "management assertion vs short-seller trade data — the truth is confidential; the exposure is real either way because supply must scale 5–10× for the growth plan." Q2 · How real is the "25 GW/yr" supply-chain visibility claim? Verdict (med-high): It's a scandium supply-chain claim, not a factory-capacity claim — and it first appeared in Bloom's defensive blog/8-K. The COO blog says the "current diversified global supply chain of scandium oxide can support up to 25 GW per year of production capacity." Factory reality is unchanged: ~1 GW run-rate → 2 GW end-2026 → ~5 GW ceiling in existing shells (June Q3 answer stands). So 25 GW describes raw-material headroom, deployed rhetorically to counter the scarcity narrative. Don't cite it as capacity guidance. Q3 · How does the $25B Brookfield framework actually work? Verdict (med on structure, high on implication): Still a financing framework , not orders. Public detail is thin: funds come from Brookfield's AI Infrastructure Fund (Nov 2025, $100B target) to "finance power projects for AI infrastructure" using Bloom systems — project financing, not general corporate lending. No disclosed take-or-pay, GW commitment, or JV economics for the expansion. The original $5B deal ran through JVs Bloom co-owns (equity-method, cost-plus pricing, margin split undisclosed) — no indication the mechanics changed, so the June Q9 revenue-quality caveat now applies to a 5× larger channel. Analysts also flagged the optics: coverage noted a ~40:1 backlog-to-RPO ratio in the wake of the short report. _Non-public:_ JV equity splits, per-project offtake terms, whether Bloom co-invests in the expanded framework. Q4 · RPO: $441M or $492M? (verification) Verdict (resolved, high): Both numbers are correct — different scopes. Q1 2026 10-Q: $441.1M product + installation RPO plus $51.5M service RPO = $492.6M total as of Mar 31, 2026. Hunterbrook quotes the total; our site quotes product+install. Action: clarify the site copy to "~$441M of firm product orders (~$493M including service)" so it can't be called stale or cherry-picked. What this changes for the thesis The short report doesn't introduce a new risk — it weaponizes two we already carry (backlog-vs-RPO, input concentration) with fresh trade-data specifics. The Brookfield expansion cuts both ways and now dominates both the bull case (demand + capital validation) and the bear case (5× more revenue routed through a financed, undisclosed-margin channel). Scandium graduates from a footnote (FEOC) to a named, dated risk with a live disclosure fight. Net: thesis unchanged — "real franchise, unforgiving price" — but the risk section is now the most current part of the pitch. Sources Bloom COO blog (Jul 7–8, 2026): https://www.bloomenergy.com/blog/demystifying-scandium-oxide-why-it-matters-in-bloom-fuel-cells/ Hunterbrook, "Bloom's Big Lie" (Jul 8, 2026): https://newsletter.hntrbrk.com/p/blooms-big-lie Bloom 8-K rebuttal (Jul 9, 2026): https://www.stocktitan.net/sec-filings/BE/8-k-bloom-energy-corp-reports-material-event-fcbd2d56d6b6.html Scandium market data: https://investingnews.com/daily/resource-investing/critical-metals-investing/scandium-production-the-problem-and-the-opportunity/ Rio Tinto Sorel-Tracy expansion (9 t/yr): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/863064/000086306425000023/form6k2025nov.htm Brookfield $25B release (Jun 30, 2026): https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260630023022/en/ Backlog/RPO analyst coverage: https://ts2.tech/en/bloom-energy-stock-slips-5-as-analysts-flag-40-to-1-backlog-concern/ Crossroads Capital follow-on attack: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/stock-ends-higher-bloom-energy-232227092.html

id: 03 Drafts/Section 4.7 - Tax Credit Stack
title: 4.7 The tax credit stack: fuel cells were the OBBBA winner
text: 4.7 The tax credit stack: fuel cells were the OBBBA winner Source: P.L. 119-21 (OBBBA), Treasury/IRS guidance, Crux 2025 H2 market report In 2022, the Biden administration passed the Inflation Reduction Act, which handed clean energy companies hundreds of billions in tax breaks. The big question heading into 2025 was whether the second Trump administration would rip those credits up. For fuel cells, the answer came in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025 , and Bloom came out ahead. The headline rule for Bloom is the federal Investment Tax Credit, which lets a buyer (or a tax-equity partner) deduct 30% of the cost of every fuel cell system they install straight off their federal tax bill. Wind and solar had to clear extra hoops to keep that 30%, like paying union-scale wages and proving zero emissions. Fuel cells were carved out and skip both, so even Bloom's natural-gas units qualify for the full 30%. The credit also lasts much longer than it does for wind or solar: 100% of the credit is available through 2033, then it tapers (75% in 2034, 50% in 2035, gone in 2036) . Wind and solar by contrast have to break ground by July 4, 2026, or get nothing. JPMorgan upgraded BE and raised its FY26 EBITDA estimate to ~$420M after the bill passed. The catch: bonus credits for building in coal-country zip codes or using American-made parts were not extended to fuel cells, so 30% is the ceiling. Three other credits round out the stack. First, customers who use Bloom's reverse-mode units to make hydrogen can claim up to $3 per kilogram produced , though the construction deadline was pulled forward to 1/1/2028. Bloom's SOEC unit hits ~80% efficiency with waste heat and is best positioned to hit the top tier. Second, the carbon capture credit pays $85/t industrial CO₂ or $180/t DAC , and Bloom's Chart partnership monetizes this directly because SOFC exhaust is 10× more concentrated . Third, and importantly for Bloom's business model, companies that can't use credits themselves can sell them for ~93¢ on the dollar in a market that grew 48% YoY to $42B. State rebates layer on top, most notably California's SGIP program ($0.60/W for >1 MW fuel cells). Bottom line: the 30% federal credit translates to ~$2,250/kW capex relief, or ~3¢/kWh levelized , which closes the gap between unsubsidized $0.13-0.16/kWh and the ~$0.10/kWh commercial grid average. The real 2026 risk is FEOC / 'material-assistance' foreign-entity rules effective 1/1/2026 , which apply to fuel-cell components and put supply-chain compliance in the analyst's crosshairs for 2026-2027.

id: 03 Drafts/Revenue Driver Tree (companion)
title: Revenue Driver Tree — the forecast architecture
text: Revenue Driver Tree — the forecast architecture Companion analysis to the Annotated Fact Base. This is the skeleton the forecast hangs on: the revenue engine that later feeds valuation (WACC/DCF out of scope here). Academic use; not investment advice. The central design choice Revenue splits at the top into two channels that grow on different logic and are modeled as separate stacks, then summed: Revenue = Arm's-length (third-party) + Affiliate (Brookfield Fund-JV) This split matters because the reported Q1 2026 headline of ~130% growth collapses to ~17% once the affiliate channel is removed. The Q1 2026 split: $377.8M arm's-length (+16.9% YoY) vs $373.3M affiliate [D]/[R]. Mashing them together is exactly how a 130% quarter fools you into reading a 17% one. 01 — Product, arm's-length: the engine The arm's-length Product line is where the business compounds. It is a volume × price node: Product = MW added × ASP MW added = min(Bookings, Capacity) Bookings = Serviceable market × Win-rate Near term the binding constraint is capacity (demand runs well past the ~1 GW line), so the schedule to 2 GW by YE2026, expandable to 5 GW, sets the ceiling [R]/[E]. Later, as capacity catches up, bookings become the constraint — the driver that matters flips partway through the horizon. Calibrate ASP to historical Product revenue ÷ implied MW shipped, then trend it. Base/bull/bear are driven by win-rate and ASP, not an invented growth rate. 02 — Installation, Service, Electricity Installation — the loss-leader. Priced to win the box, not to make money; gross margin has been negative every year on record. Modeled as an attach onto Product MW: Installation = Attach% × Product_MW × Install_$/MW . Hold margin in its historical negative band. Service — the durable floor. The annuity and lowest-variance line. Driven by cumulative deployed capacity , not current-year sales: Cum_GW = prior Cum_GW + MW_added; Service = Cum_GW × Service_$/GW·yr . Factor ≈ $127M per GW-year (FY2025 service revenue / cumulative GW) [D]; margin ≈ 13% [R]. Electricity — the run-off. Legacy own-and-operate PPA fleet, deliberately shrinking. A decay line, not a growth line; don't extrapolate its margin swings (one PPA write-down drove a −117% year). 03 — The affiliate channel, and its drag Product sold into the Brookfield Fund-JVs is real revenue, but it is gated by how fast Brookfield deploys capital under the $5B / five-year framework — of which only a small fraction is funded so far (~$11.4M committed-but-unfunded at Q1 2026) [R]. It is a scenario switch, not a trend : do not assume the full $5B converts on a smooth curve. Two contra-effects ride with it, both scaling with affiliate shipments (not arm's-length sales): the equity-method loss Bloom books on the JVs' losses, and the deferred profit it cannot recognize while it part-owns the buyer. On the tree these are the dashed contra — a deduction from the affiliate channel's net contribution, not from consolidated revenue. 04 — The four assumptions that move the answer A driver tree is only as honest as the handful of inputs the valuation turns on: 1. Win-rate and 2. ASP trajectory set the arm's-length top line. 3. Brookfield deployment pace sets the size and durability of the affiliate channel (the primary base/bull/bear lever). 4. Terminal margin — must be defended against the segment table, since blended margin only improves because mix keeps tilting toward Product. Capacity factor and gas price sit in the cost work but feed the win-rate link (delivered cost) and the Electricity segment. Key anchors used here Capacity ~1 GW today → 2 GW by YE2026, expandable to 5 GW [R]/[E]; Brookfield $5B/5-yr framework, small funded fraction (~$11.4M unfunded at Q1 2026) [R]; segment gross margins (Product ~35%, Installation negative, Service ~13% FY2025, Electricity volatile) [R]; Q1 2026 split $377.8M arm's-length (+16.9%) vs $373.3M affiliate [D]/[R]; service annuity ≈ $127M per GW-year [D]. Tiers: [R] reported, [D] derived, [E] estimate.

id: 03 Drafts/Section 2.5 - Business Moat
title: 2.5 The business moat: why no SOFC competitor can catch up at gigawatt scale
text: 2.5 The business moat: why no SOFC competitor can catch up at gigawatt scale Source: Bloom FY2025 10-K, competitor filings, third-party analysis The chemistry edge in section 2 explains why SOFC beats PEM and MCFC on paper. It does not explain why no other SOFC maker has caught up. The honest answer is that Bloom has been building the factory and putting boxes in the ground since 2008 , while every credible Western competitor is still measured in pilots. As of YE 2025, Bloom had ~ 1.8 GW cumulatively deployed across 1,200+ sites in nine countries , generating $2.02B of FY2025 revenue (+37% YoY) on a $6B product backlog (2.5× YoY) and ~$20B total backlog . The nearest Western SOFC peers are an order of magnitude smaller. Doosan Fuel Cell only began SOFC mass production in July 2025 at 50 MW/year using Ceres-licensed tech . Bosch exited SOFC entirely in February 2025 and laid off 550 staff. Mitsubishi's MEGAMIE is still a 250 kW Japan-only product . Bloom's Fremont line is at 1 GW/year today, on track for 2 GW by YE 2026, expandable to 5 GW , roughly 20× any single Western competitor. The deal sheet matches the factory: Brookfield $5B / 1 GW for behind-the-meter AI power , AEP up to 1 GW / ~$2.65B , Oracle 2.8 GW , SK ecoplant 500 MW / $4.5B , Equinix 100+ MW , and Nebius $2.6B / 250 MW guaranteed . The thread: technology gets a competitor in the room, but a decade of installed base, a manufacturing footprint, and customer concentration in the hyperscalers actually buying gigawatts today is the real moat. The honest counter: top three customers were 53% of FY2024 revenue (#1 is the SK related party) . Bloom has carried a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit and has never sustainably earned its cost of capital . And on a gas feedstock, lifecycle emissions are roughly 679–839 lb CO₂/MWh, at parity with a modern gas turbine, not zero . The moat is the head start, not the molecule.

id: 03 Drafts/Efficiency Fuel and R&D Pipeline (companion)
title: Efficiency, Fuel, and the R&D Pipeline
text: Efficiency, Fuel, and the R&D Pipeline Companion analysis to the Annotated Fact Base — answers to the open questions on efficiency, the fuel bill, the 800°C heat, and R&D. Academic use; not investment advice. 01 — What the efficiency numbers mean η = electricity out ÷ fuel energy in ; gas per MWh = 3.412 MMBtu ÷ η . Three numbers circulate and measure different things: | Figure | What it measures | |---|---| | 54% | Average lifetime electrical efficiency on natural gas, degradation included | | 60% | Electrical efficiency on 100% hydrogen at the R&D facility | | 90% | Combined electricity plus captured, used heat (CHP) — not applicable to data centers | The trap is stacking the 90% against the 54%. The 90% is not electrical efficiency — it counts heat recovered only when something next door uses it. On gas with the heat vented, the box is a 54% machine. (Note: the site's "53–65%" is the Server 6.5 datasheet nameplate range; 54% is the honest lifetime, real-world figure.) 02 — The fuel bill, and whether the boxes need a gas line Yes — every box needs a gas line. "Off-grid" means off the electric grid, not off fuel. The customer pays a gas utility every month, on top of buying the box and paying Bloom to service it. At 54%: 3.412 ÷ 0.54 = 6.32 MMBtu of gas per MWh . | Natural gas price | Fuel $/MWh @ 54% | Fuel $/MWh @ 40% genset | Efficiency saving | |---|---|---|---| | $3.50/MMBtu (Henry Hub, mid-2026) | $22 | $30 | $8 | | $5.00/MMBtu (delivered, incl. transport) | $32 | $43 | $11 | Project Jupiter (Oracle) makes the fuel bill physical: up to 2.45 GW fed by a fast-tracked 16-mile, 400 MMcf/day pipeline. 2.45 GW at 54% needs ~372,000 MMBtu/day against the line's ~414,000. | Project Jupiter | Figure | |---|---| | Bloom capacity | up to 2.45 GW [R] | | Gas throughput | up to 400 MMcf/day ≈ 151M MMBtu/yr [R]/[D] | | Annual gas bill | ~$530M ($3.50) to ~$760M ($5.00) [D] | | One-time equipment | ~$18B (2.45 GW × ~$7,500/kW) [D] | | Fuel saved vs 40% genset | ~$185M–$265M / yr [D] | | Cumulative fuel, 15–20 yr | ~$8B–$15B [D] | The key takeaway: fuel is only $22–32 of Bloom's ~$121/MWh all-in cost (≈$95 with the 30% ITC). The box and stack-replacement service are the other four-fifths. Higher efficiency shrinks only the fuel slice — which is why Bloom undercuts an inefficient on-site genset on fuel yet still costs more than grid power overall. Hydrogen makes the fuel line ~10× worse (~$200/MWh at $4–6/kg), which is why no site runs on it yet. 03 — Capturing the 800°C heat The module runs ~800°C internally and exhausts heat averaging >350°C — high-grade where a PEM cell's sub-100°C heat is not. Captured and used, this heat is the only thing that turns the 54% electrical figure into the 90% combined one. The data-center use that matters is cooling : routing exhaust through an absorption chiller displaces electric chillers, with management claiming a 10–15% PUE gain . Caveats: the chiller is added capital that only pays where a thermal load sits next to the box; Bloom's heat figures are modeled to ±5%, not field-validated; >350°C is well short of turbine-grade. Treat it as a win-rate input, not a revenue line. 04 — R&D spend Reported only in aggregate (no project-level breakout). Per-project budgets quoted in third-party summaries are invented. | Period | R&D ($000s) | R&D % of revenue | |---|---|---| | FY2021 | 103,396 | 10.6% | | FY2022 | 150,606 | 12.6% | | FY2023 | 155,865 | 11.7% | | FY2024 | 148,629 | 10.1% | | FY2025 | 185,993 | 9.2% | | Q1 2026 | 56,849 | 7.6% | Dollars rose ~80% (FY21→FY25) while falling from 10.6% to 7.6% of revenue — operating leverage on a commercialized platform, not rising research intensity. Model R&D as a declining % of revenue. 05 — Technology directions (disclosed) Capacity scale-up — ~1 GW today; 2 GW by end-2026, expandable to 5 GW; ~$100M for the next GW [R]/[E]. Native 800V DC output — all new shipments DC-ready; aligns with NVIDIA's 2027 800V DC racks [R]/[E]. SOEC electrolyzer (hydrogen) — same platform in reverse; ~2 GW/yr line at Newark since 2022, INL-validated as most efficient commercial-scale electrolyzer measured. Gated by the $3/kg H₂ credit requiring construction start by Jan 2028 [R]. Carbon capture (CCUS) — concentrated exhaust makes capture cheaper than a turbine's; Chart Industries JV targets the $85/ton credit [R]. Both hydrogen and carbon are subsidy-linked terminal-value optionality, not near-term drivers. 06 — Claims to keep OUT of the model (corrections) Three external/AI summaries introduced items that don't survive sourcing: No Bloom "Khyber/Rubin" or "Direct-Conversion Architectures" project — Rubin and Kyber are NVIDIA's. The "10× capacity, constant headcount" line traces to nothing — the disclosed claim is 2 GW by end-2026, expandable to 5 GW. "Quanta" is a customer (Quanta Computer, expanded an install 150% in Nov 2024), not a microgrid project; the flagship microgrid is Oracle's Jupiter. "70–80% repeat customers" overstates management's "over two-thirds." 07 — How this maps to the driver tree None of this is a revenue line. Efficiency, heat capture, and 800V DC feed win-rate ; the gas-line requirement and fuel bill are a customer operating-cost / siting constraint ; capacity scale-up feeds the MW cap ; hydrogen and carbon are subsidy-linked terminal optionality . The only forecast input here is aggregate R&D , projected as a declining % of revenue.

id: 03 Drafts/Formulas Rates and Units Key (companion)
title: Formulas, Rates, and Units — the key
text: Formulas, Rates, and Units — the key Companion key to the Driver Tree and Fact Base: every equation and $/unit notation used across the documents, written out. Tiers: [R] reported, [D] derived, [E] estimate. Formulas (the driver tree + efficiency math) | Formula | Meaning / typical value | |---|---| | Revenue = Arm's-length + Affiliate | Top of the tree; the two channels are forecast separately and summed. Reported Q1'26 growth of 130% is ~17% arm's-length [D]. | | Product = MW × ASP | The arm's-length engine; the core volume × price node. | | MW added = min(Bookings, Capacity) | Binding constraint flips: capacity-limited near term, demand-limited later. | | Bookings = Serviceable market × Win-rate | Feeds MW added once capacity is no longer the cap. | | Installation = Attach% × Product_MW × Install_$/MW | Loss-leader tied to product volume; held at historical negative margin. | | Cum.GW = prior Cum.GW + MW added | Cumulative installed base; drives the Service annuity. | | Service = Cum.GW × Service_$/GW·yr | Durable recurring line; factor ≈ $127M/GW-yr [D]. | | Affiliate = JV_MW × ASP | Gated by Brookfield's deployment pace; a scenario switch, not a trend. | | ASP ≈ Product revenue ÷ implied MW | Grounds the ASP input before it is trended. | | η = electricity out ÷ fuel energy in | Electrical efficiency: 54% on natural gas [R]. | | Gas per MWh = 3.412 MMBtu ÷ η | At 54%: 6.32 MMBtu/MWh [D]. | Rates and units | Unit | How it applies / typical value | |---|---| | $/kW | Equipment ≈ $7,500/kW [E]; 30% ITC ≈ $2,250/kW [D]; ASP quoted per kW. | | $/MWh | All-in delivered cost ≈ $121/MWh (≈ $95 with 30% ITC) [D]; fuel slice $22–32/MWh [D]. | | $/GW-yr | Service annuity factor ≈ $127M/GW-yr [D]. | | $/MMBtu | Henry Hub ≈ $3.50; delivered ≈ $5.00 [E]. | | $/kg | H₂ fuel ≈ $4–6/kg (≈$200/MWh) [E]; $3/kg production credit [R]. | | $/ton | Carbon-capture credit $85/ton ($180/ton if pulled from air) [R]. | | $/W | California SGIP rebate ≈ $0.60/W of fuel-cell capacity [R]. | | % of revenue | Capex 3.5% of revenue [R]; gross margins (Product ≈35%, Service ≈13%). | | MWh / MMBtu | 1 MWh = 3.412 MMBtu [R]; 6.32 MMBtu makes 1 MWh at 54% [D]. | | MMcf/day | Pipeline throughput; the Jupiter line moves up to 400 MMcf/day ≈ 151M MMBtu/yr [R]/[D]. | Related shorthand ASP — average selling price, the Product node price, quoted in $/kW. LCOE — levelized cost of energy; the all-in $/MWh metric (≈$121/MWh unsubsidized) [D]. ITC — 30% Investment Tax Credit on installed cost; trims delivered cost ≈$26/MWh [D]. RPO — remaining performance obligations; contracted unbilled backlog (Product $441.1M at Q1'26) [R]. PUE — power usage effectiveness; heat capture claims a 10–15% gain. CHP — combined heat and power; electricity plus captured, used heat (the 90% combined figure). All-in cost, fuel, and credit values swing most on gas price, capacity factor, and the efficiency assumption. Academic use; not investment advice.

id: 03 Drafts/Section 06 - Bear Thesis
title: 06 The bear thesis: what could go wrong
text: 06 The bear thesis: what could go wrong Source: Hindenburg Research, Bloom 10-K filings, short interest data A balanced read has to acknowledge the other side. The most prominent bear case is Hindenburg Research's September 2019 short report , which argued the Energy Server's economics were materially worse than disclosed: real-world efficiency degraded from 58.3% at install to ~51% after 25 months, and average stack life was 4.8 to 5.2 years against >5-year warranties, meaning service costs were structurally underreserved. Bloom disputed the report, but the underlying physics (ceramic stacks degrade with use and must be replaced) is the same "double-edged sword" already flagged in section 1. The financials add fuel. Bloom carries a ~$4.0B accumulated deficit and has never sustainably earned its cost of capital after ~17 years. Share count is up roughly 23% over the past year between two large convertibles, so equity holders are absorbing real dilution as the company scales. Top three customers were 53% of FY2024 revenue with #1 being a related party . And valuation is rich: BE trades at ~80× book value and an EV/EBITDA above 600× , with short interest near 10% of float as of January 2026 . The bull case requires backlog conversion, lasting subsidies, manageable degradation, and easing concentration. None of those are facts yet. Worth flagging now, worth a deeper look later.

id: 01 Sources/Energy Industry Macro Backdrop Joint BE CEG Chapter
title: Energy Industry Macro Backdrop — Joint BE & CEG Chapter
text: Energy Industry Macro Backdrop — Joint BE & CEG Chapter High-level environment chapter shared by the Bloom (BE) and Constellation (CEG) initiations for the joint class presentation. Deliberately contains no valuation or price content; company-specific transmission lives in each team's own macro analysis. Pinned 2026-07-14. Key takeaways Three historical eras of US gas/power: scarcity 1997–2008 (HH $2.49 → $8.86); shale abundance + flat load 2009–2020 (~$3.25 avg, merchant nuclear squeezed); shock & inflection 2021–2025 (2022 crisis, IRA → OBBBA, AI ends the flat-load era). Six defining features of mid-2026: first structural demand growth in ~two decades (PJM attributes nearly all of its peak-forecast increase to data centers); scarcity priced but administratively capped; gas abundant but claimed by LNG exports; policy is explicit industrial policy (OBBBA winners face a compliance regime, not a cliff); financing two-faced (easy policy rate, 30Y > 5%); physical/political friction now binding. Forecast to 2030: 82 → 153 GW data-center demand; 253 GW of gas in queues (+86% y/y); turbine backlogs 3–7 years; HH ~$3.50 through 2027; four open risks led by the durability of AI capex. The mirror: most macro shocks move BE and CEG in opposite directions through the same mechanism (gas price, grid scarcity) — the point of the combined presentation. Shared discipline: one pinned date, one frozen source set, shared scenario coordinates, one demand pie both teams' claimed gigawatts must fit inside. Useful mainly for the joint presentation; the BE-side transmission is carried by BE Macro Analysis Complete v2 .

id: 01 Sources/BE Complete Model Workbook v5
title: BE Complete Model Workbook v5 (BE_complete.xlsx, July 2026)
text: BE Complete Model Workbook v5 (BE_complete.xlsx, July 2026) The finished financial model — the file the team's target prices derive from. Tabs: DCF, Sensitivity, Reverse DCF, WACC calculation, Financial Statement Model, Ratios, Return data for CAPM, Stacked Historicals, Changelog v5. (BE_REVERSE DCF.xlsx is the identical build minus the Sensitivity tab.) Key takeaways Headline outputs (pinned 2026-06-27, price $257.98): perpetuity DCF $8.60 /share, EBITDA-exit (13.5×) $27.87 /share, 50/50 blended $18.23 /share → implied downside ≈ −93% vs the pinned price. WACC 21.03% from a regression beta of 3.7703 (Rf 4.3%, MRP 4.6% → cost of equity 21.64%); capital structure ~96.6% equity, so the WACC dispute is entirely a beta dispute. Forecast: revenue $2.02B → $9.34B FY2026–FY2030 (70/45/30/22/18% growth), UFCF margins −2.26 → 6.86% after the v5 tax-sign fix. v5 audit fixes (Changelog): forecast tax negation removed (+5% rate, removes a phantom tax benefit); Gordon terminal FCF normalized (ΔNWC at g × terminal NWC → normalized numerator $939.1M); per-share divisor moved from basic 284,440k to diluted 319,700k (−11% per share); valuation date pinned to the price date; FY2025 capex −$56.8M restored. Reverse DCF tab: at the model's own 21% WACC and a generous 20% terminal FCF margin, today's EV ($82.7B at the pin) requires g₁ ≈ 124% , fading to 3.5%, reaching ~$210B FY2036 revenue — 5.6× the disclosed 5 GW capacity ceiling ($37.5B at $7.5B/GW). Even a heroic 25% margin needs 4.4×. Sensitivity tab: beta ladder 1.77 → 5.77 maps to blended $30.90 → $12.32; every cell across WACC × multiple and WACC × growth grids sits far below market. id: 01 Sources/BE Reverse DCF Experiments Annex
title: BE Reverse DCF Experiments Annex (Results, valuation date 2026-07-14)
text: BE Reverse DCF Experiments Annex (Results, valuation date 2026-07-14) Discharges section 11.3 of the Macro Analysis v2 with computed figures: a faithful reconstruction of the workbook's reverse-DCF specification, run across discount rates. Applying the 20% terminal FCF margin from year one is generous to the market case, so every figure is a lower bound on the burden the price implies. Key takeaways Pin re-pull: BE closed $243.40 on 2026-07-14; market cap $69.2B on 284.44M shares; EV $69.4B (converts at principal) or $72.0B if-converted — the choice moves no conclusion. Rate–growth frontier (implied path at the pinned EV): 8.0% → 0.7× the 5 GW ceiling; 9.5% → 1.0× (the ceiling binds exactly) ; 10.5% → 1.2×; 12% → 1.5×; 15% → 2.4×; 18% → 3.4×; 21% → 4.7× the ceiling, 3.3× the entire behind-the-meter pie, 98% of ALL new US data-center load . Verdict confirmed with exact figures: at 21% the market-implied path is infeasible on all three bounds simultaneously; at the bottom-up 10.5% cost of equity the burden falls by three quarters (fits inside the BTM pie at 0.8×, still 1.2× the disclosed roadmap). The overvaluation finding is not rate-robust — the honest conclusion is the frontier itself. The most presentable single number: the current price is internally consistent only if Bloom is discounted like a mature industrial (~9.5%, against a regression beta several times higher) and grows ~68% in year one, fades to 3%, and ships at its full disclosed capacity ceiling in perpetuity at a 20% FCF margin — against a current operating margin of 3.6%. New flag from the re-pull: the Hunterbrook short report (Jul 7–8, 2026) intersects the C5/C6 supply-chain findings and must be evaluated against the fact base before the exam. id: 01 Sources/BE Macro Analysis Complete v2
title: BE Macro Analysis Complete v2 (all phases executed, pinned 2026-07-14)
text: BE Macro Analysis Complete v2 (all phases executed, pinned 2026-07-14) The complete macro workstream: six transmission channels (AI capex/demand, rates & credit, natural gas, power markets & scarcity, policy, manufacturing inputs), each wired to named model nodes. Every figure traces to a frozen primary source (freeze archive v4). Key takeaways Pinned inputs (2026-07-14): UST 10Y 4.58%, 30Y 5.08%, 10Y real 2.33%; Fed funds 3.50–3.75%; Damodaran implied ERP 4.45%; HY OAS ~2.7%; Henry Hub spot $3.29. The macro configuration priced into the stock: power scarcity persists, gas stays cheap vs grid power, capital keeps flowing, the ITC survives. Verdict: favorable on four of five pillars — the equity price is the only element the macro evidence cannot support at the model's own discount rate. C1: hyperscalers guide ~$725B 2026 capex (+77%); Cleanview measures the behind-the-meter pie at 30% of new data-center load with Bloom's OEM share at 14% — adopted as the model's win-rate anchor; conversion reality: 1.5 GW operating vs 56 GW announced. C2: at ~96% equity weight the WACC collapses to the cost of equity: CoE = 4.58% + β × 4.45%. Regression β ≈ 3.77 → 21%; bottom-up β 1.1–1.4 → 9.5–10.8%. Settled empirically by the rate-robustness experiment rather than by silent choice. C3: fuel cost keys to the datasheet HHV band — $6.32/MWh per $1/MMBtu at the 54% lifetime anchor; three-case gas $2.50 / $3.60 / $6.50. C4: PJM 2027/28 BRA cleared at the FERC cap $333.44/MW-day vs $529.80 simulated uncapped; first footprint-wide reliability shortfall; grid comparison line updates to $89/MWh (+$15 capacity adder). C5: policy risk migrated from repeal to FEOC compliance (MACR 40%→60%, two-tier look-up, indemnities landing on Bloom's balance sheet); Jupiter pipeline block reported in New Mexico (verification flagged). C6: product gross margin ~35% held FY2023–Q1 2026 — the cost-down curve has matched input inflation; capex 3.5% of revenue is the specified falsifier if it steps up. Bear coherence: an AI-capex contraction softens power prices, widens credit, slows Brookfield, and shrinks the conversion pie — one state of the world, not independent shocks. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Overvaluation finding is not rate-robust
title: The overvaluation finding is not rate-robust — the honest conclusion is the frontier
text: The overvaluation finding is not rate-robust — the honest conclusion is the frontier At the workbook's 21% rate the market-implied path is infeasible on all three bounds at once: 4.7× the disclosed factory ceiling, 3.3× the entire measured behind-the-meter segment, and 98% of every gigawatt of new US data-center load (2025–28 build rate). But at the bottom-up ~10.5% cost of equity the burden falls by three quarters: the path fits inside the behind-the-meter pie (0.8×) while still exceeding the 5 GW roadmap (1.2×) — a boundary case, not an impossibility. The market price embeds jointly a low discount rate and a successful hypergrowth path; neither alone justifies it at 21%, and at 10.5% it requires capacity beyond current disclosure or a longer runway. The paper says so: the verdict is presented as a (discount rate, growth) frontier rather than a point overvaluation call. This also disciplines the site's framing — the buy-trigger math at a market-level 10.5% rate is the defensible form of the claim. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Capacity ceiling binds at 9.5 percent discount rate
title: The disclosed 5 GW capacity ceiling binds exactly near a 9.5% discount rate
text: The disclosed 5 GW capacity ceiling binds exactly near a 9.5% discount rate The single most presentable number of the reverse-DCF workstream: at the 2026-07-14 pin (EV ≈ $69.4B), the market-implied revenue path exactly fills the disclosed 5 GW/yr capacity ceiling when the discount rate is ~ 9.5% — implying 67.9% year-one growth and $37.3B FY2036 revenue. The current price is therefore internally consistent only if Bloom is simultaneously discounted like a mature industrial (~9.5% or below, against a measured regression beta several times higher) and assumed to grow ~68% in year one, fade to 3%, and ship at its full disclosed capacity ceiling in perpetuity at a 20% FCF margin — against a current operating margin of 3.6%. Every element of that sentence is a named, testable assumption, which is what makes it the exam-ready statement of the finding. id: 02 Atomic Notes/PJM cleared at FERC cap vs 530 simulated
title: PJM's capacity auction cleared at its FERC cap — 60% below the simulated uncapped price
text: PJM's capacity auction cleared at its FERC cap — 60% below the simulated uncapped price PJM's 2027/2028 Base Residual Auction (Dec 17, 2025) cleared at the FERC-approved cap of $333.44/MW-day footprint-wide , while PJM's own simulation puts the uncapped price near $529.80 . The RTO fell 6,623 MW short of its reliability requirement for the first time on record, and PJM attributes nearly all of the peak-forecast increase (~5,100 of 5,250 MW) to data centers. Read-through: the marginal price of firm capacity sits at an administrative ceiling, the scarcity is officially attributed to Bloom's own demand class, and the print converts to ~$14.6/MWh for a flat load (updating the model's grid comparison to $89/MWh retail + $15 capacity adder). The grid alternative reprices upward on scarcity while Bloom's cost rides a learning curve — the lines converge from both sides. The queue evidence (gas capacity +86% to 253 GW, reforms clearing deadwood) also dates the fade: the rent is real today and its erosion has visibly begun, which is what defends the H-model fade structure. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Cleanview 14 percent win-rate anchor
title: Cleanview measures Bloom at 14% of tracked behind-the-meter projects — adopted as the model's win-rate anchor
text: Cleanview measures Bloom at 14% of tracked behind-the-meter projects — adopted as the model's win-rate anchor Cleanview's tracker identifies 56 GW of planned behind-the-meter capacity across 46 data-center projects (~30% of all planned US data-center capacity, 90% announced in 2025); ~75% of identified equipment is gas combustion, and OEM shares of tracked projects run Caterpillar 33%, Bloom 14% (Bloom rising on the Oracle Jupiter award). The 14% is adopted as the external calibration anchor for the model's win-rate input — converting its most subjective assumption into one with an independent empirical reference. Sanity check: 14% of a ~7.1 GW/yr behind-the-meter pie ≈ 1.0 GW/yr, coherent with Bloom's ~1 GW manufacturing line and 0.43 GW shipped in 2025. The serviceable market is wired as load additions × 30% BTM share, both sourced and toggled. id: 02 Atomic Notes/WACC 21 percent is really a beta dispute
title: Bloom's discount rate is a cost of equity, and every dispute about it is a dispute about beta
text: Bloom's discount rate is a cost of equity, and every dispute about it is a dispute about beta At market values Bloom is ~96% equity and cash interest on the whole recourse stack runs ~$5M/yr, so the WACC collapses to the cost of equity within rounding: CoE = 4.58% + β × 4.45% at the pinned inputs. The workbook's 21.0% WACC is consistent with the regression β ≈ 3.77; a bottom-up β from established power-equipment makers (~1.1–1.4 unlevered) implies 9.5–10.8% . Both estimates are argued honestly: the regression window covers the period BE traded as an AI-scarcity vehicle (may measure narrative flow), but Bloom's cash flows genuinely concentrate in hyperscaler capex — among the most cyclical, market-correlated spending lines in the economy — so a high measured beta may be economically correct. Resolution: regression primary, bottom-up as disclosed sensitivity, and the dispute settled empirically by the rate-robustness experiment rather than by silent choice. Related Overvaluation finding is not rate-robust Pinned macro inputs July 14 2026 Tags #bloom #valuation #wacc #beta

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Bear case is one coherent state of the world
title: The bear case is one coherent state of the world, not independent shocks
text: The bear case is one coherent state of the world, not independent shocks An AI-capex contraction softens power prices (undoing the gas self-hedge), widens credit (the buildout is increasingly debt-financed — the demand bear and the credit bear are the same scenario), slows Brookfield deployment (a levered infrastructure decision against a 30Y above 5%), and shrinks the pie into which backlog converts. This correlation structure is the analytical spine of the scenario matrix: bear coordinates are assigned jointly (gas $6.50 with soft power prices, capacity rents collapsing, credit windows closing into the 2028–30 maturity wall, project-level MACR failures) rather than flexed one axis at a time. The single-axis elasticity table exists but states its own limitation. Structurally, near-term revenue is insulated by the capacity constraint (MW added = min(bookings, capacity)); the outer years and backlog conversion take the hit. Related Convertible stack is equity in waiting BTM conversion 1.5 GW operating vs 56 GW announced Tags #bloom #macro #scenarios

id: 02 Atomic Notes/FEOC compliance is the binding policy risk
title: The binding policy risk has migrated from ITC repeal to FEOC compliance
text: The binding policy risk has migrated from ITC repeal to FEOC compliance The 30% fuel-cell ITC survived OBBBA intact through 2033 (taper to 2036), so the live risk is now the FEOC regime (effective Jan 1, 2026; IRS Notice 2026-15): facilities starting construction in 2026 need a material-assistance cost ratio of at least 40% non-prohibited-foreign-entity content, rising to 60% by 2030 , tested two tiers up the supply chain, with a six-year assessment window and a ten-year recapture period. The intersection is citable at both ends: Bloom's FY2025 10-K discloses explicit tier-2/tier-3 exposure to Chinese rare-earth metals and compounds , and its own risk factor says misinterpreting the rules may cause partial or full loss of the credit — with customers, financiers, and equity investors possibly requiring indemnification , putting the credit risk partly on Bloom's balance sheet. The realistic near-term policy bear is project-level MACR or documentation failure with recapture and indemnity tails, not statutory repeal. Monitorables: MACR certification evidence on 2026-start projects; Treasury safe-harbor tables due Dec 31, 2026. Related FEOC material assistance rules Scandia-stabilized zirconia scandium most FEOC-sensitive input OBBBA fuel cell ITC carve-out Tags #bloom #policy #feoc #risk

id: 02 Atomic Notes/V5 audit fixes cut per-share value
title: The v5 audit fixes moved the DCF from $8.95/$32.07/$20.51 to $8.60/$27.87/$18.23
text: The v5 audit fixes moved the DCF from $8.95/$32.07/$20.51 to $8.60/$27.87/$18.23 Three fixes in the v5 changelog reshaped the per-share answer: the forecast tax-rate sign fix (a negation had been crediting a phantom tax benefit; the rate is now +5%, lowering every forecast UFCF margin — the ramp falls from −1.31→8.01% to −2.26→6.86% ), the Gordon terminal FCF normalization (ΔNWC at g × terminal NWC rather than the 18%-growth-level −$363M, raising the perpetuity terminal value to a $939.1M numerator), and the per-share divisor moving from basic 284.44M to diluted 319.7M shares (−11% per share, per the Q1 2026 10-Q). Net effect: blended value fell from $20.51 to $18.23. The fixes cut in both directions, which is what you want from an audit — the direction of each is logged with source and accession number. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Reverse DCF implied 124 percent year-one growth
title: At the model's 21% WACC, the price implies 124% year-one growth and $210B FY2036 revenue — 5.6× the capacity ceiling
text: At the model's 21% WACC, the price implies 124% year-one growth and $210B FY2036 revenue — 5.6× the capacity ceiling The workbook's Reverse DCF tab solves for the growth path that makes the pinned $257.98 price (EV $82.7B) correct: g₁ = 124%, fading linearly to 3.5% over ten years → FY2036 revenue ≈ $210B, a 59% ten-year CAGR — at a generous 20% terminal FCF margin. The disclosed 5 GW/yr capacity ceiling supports ~$37.5B of product revenue at $7.5B/GW, so the implied path is 5.6× the ceiling . The margin scenario table removes the escape hatch: even a heroic 25% cash margin needs 4.4×; a harsh 10% needs 11.9×. As the tab itself reads: there is no cell in that table a cash-flow investor can defend — either the market discounts at a far lower rate than the regression beta implies, or it is pricing an AI-scarcity option, not cash flows. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Fuel cost 6.32 per MWh per dollar of gas
title: Each $1/MMBtu of delivered gas moves Bloom's fuel cost by $6.32/MWh
text: Each $1/MMBtu of delivered gas moves Bloom's fuel cost by $6.32/MWh The model keys fuel cost to the datasheet's HHV heat-rate band directly (5,811–7,127 Btu/kWh over life; the 54% lifetime efficiency anchor implies 6,318 Btu/kWh), because gas is billed on HHV — removing the ~10% LHV-vs-HHV wedge that flatters cross-vendor comparisons. At the anchor, fuel cost = $6.32/MWh per $1/MMBtu of delivered gas ($5.81–$7.13 across the degradation band). Three-case gas coordinates, set empirically: bull $2.50 (20th percentile of trailing-decade annual means), base $3.60 (average of STEO 2026–27 forecasts), bear $6.50 (worst realized annual mean, 2022) → fuel cost $15.8 / $22.7 / $41.1 per MWh, plus a stated $0.45–1.20 delivered-basis adder. Gas risk is asymmetric upward, but partially self-hedged where gas sets the grid's marginal price. id: 02 Atomic Notes/Macro pillar scorecard four of five favorable
title: Macro pillar scorecard: favorable on four of five — the price is the only unsupported element
text: Macro pillar scorecard: favorable on four of five — the price is the only unsupported element The presentation-ready verdict of the macro workstream: Bloom's macro configuration is genuinely favorable on power scarcity (capped auction 60% below simulated clearing, first reliability miss, 4–5-year queues — a multi-year rent with a dated fade), cheap gas (benign base ~$3.50 through 2027, risk asymmetric upward but partially self-hedged), capital availability (open but not cheap; the zero-coupon window demonstrated), and the ITC (statute intact to 2033; the live risk is compliance). Demand — the borrowed pillar — is real and enormous ($725B guided, $80B power-constrained Azure backlog). The one element the macro evidence cannot support is the equity price at the model's own discount rate : the market claims more of the demand pie than exists at 21%, and a boundary share at a generous rate. Falsifiers specified in advance: sustained arm's-length growth above mid-teens; a quarter of operating cash flow not carried by affiliate collections; MACR certification evidence; primary-source resolution of the Jupiter block; the next gigawatt of capacity landing near $100M. Related Overvaluation finding is not rate-robust Q1 2026 130 percent growth is 17 percent ex-affiliate Tags #bloom #macro #verdict

id: 02 Atomic Notes/Convertible stack is equity in waiting
title: The convertible stack is equity in waiting — ~21.6M potential shares, and a 2030 event keyed to $194.97
text: The convertible stack is equity in waiting — ~21.6M potential shares, and a 2030 event keyed to $194.97 From the FY2025 10-K Note 8: total recourse debt $2,613.7M, all convertible. The residual Green Notes (conv. $18.85 and $20.84) are more than eleven times in the money (~8.7M if-converted shares, capped-call protection exhausted at a $26.46 cap one-ninth of the price), and the $2.5B 0% notes (conv. $194.97) crossed into the money in H1 2026 (+12.8M shares). Total ~21.6M potential shares, ~7.6% of basic. Cash interest is a rounding item; the real cost of the zero-coupon capital is the share-delivery obligation — the FY2025 exchange cycle ($66.2M inducement, $32.3M extinguishment) is the realized precedent that convertible capital here is cheap in coupon and expensive in exit. The balance-sheet exposure is a 2030 refinancing event whose severity depends entirely on the share price relative to $194.97 , sitting inside a 2028–2030 maturity sequence that depends on the issuance window staying open. title: Completed model values BE at $18.23 blended vs the $257.98 pinned price (−93%)
text: Completed model values BE at $18.23 blended vs the $257.98 pinned price (−93%) The finished v5 workbook prints $8.60/share on the perpetuity DCF, $27.87 on the 13.5× EBITDA exit, and $18.23 blended 50/50 — against the pinned 2026-06-27 price of $257.98, an implied downside of ~93%. These are the group's model-derived target prices at the model's own 21.03% WACC and diluted 319.7M shares. The gap between the two legs is itself informative: the exit-multiple leg embeds a 17.3% implied terminal growth rate, so even the "friendly" leg is far below market. The number the market pays is not reachable by discounting the forecast — which is why the presentation-ready finding is the reverse-DCF frontier, not a point target.
